passages.name: Act1Epilogue
passages.content:
You rush home, not stopping and not looking back until you are behind your deadlocked front door. Only then do you find yourself able to breath without gasping.

The image of Stitch’s ice cold stare is burned into your brain and won’t leave. You mull over all he said. The Prom. The pithy curses. His amulet.

Your thoughts keep returning to that amulet. You felt it immediately. Something like you’ve never experienced. Something that overshadowed your own powers. Something that voids any progress you’ve made stopping what’s happening at Charlton.

If you can’t use magic on them, what can you do? The police certainly aren’t going to believe a Headmaster of a school is using magic to brainwash his students.

 

 Then there are the threats. Could he really turn you into a stripper? Working in some sleazy bar. What did he call it, Dicks? Somewhere men call you names.

 

 *And would it be you, or would it be Emily?*

 

 Both options send a shudder coursing through you. 

 

 You think back to the curses and how meekly Christina agreed to correct them. All that effort, wasted. And to think, he expects you to come to him willingly.

 

 Then there is his offer. You reflect on how easily you accepted. How you willingly agreed to attend that secret meeting. If there’s one silver lining, it’s that you might be able to learn more about their plans. And stop them. If you still want to.

 

 Your thoughts turn to Christina. The memories that you gave her would have solidified by now. She no doubt has woken up as a brand new person. A small, meek thing, clumsy and unpopular. Everything that Christina would have hated. And you did that to her.

 

 Then there is his arrogance that you will come around. He didn’t even react to your challenge. Is he really so confident that you can do so little to stop him?

Lost in your thoughts for an hour, you admit the day is wasted. You’re too preoccupied to do anything else today.

Actions

<>

passages.name: CheerleaderIntervention1
passages.content:
“I’m just checking on the Ward in here,” you whisper to her. “Making sure no one has tampered with it.”

 

 She looks at you with a confused look. “I thought I was checking in here,” she mumbles.

 

 You gesture holding up your hands. “I didn’t know we had set Wards.”

 

 Stacey gives you a look, but shrugs her shoulders. “Alright. So long as its done by someone. Chrissie will go mad if we miss something.”

 

 “I’ll double-check,” you reply.

 

 Stacey smiles politely, then turns and walks out. You sigh with relief. 

 “Fall asleep,” you mutter, “and let your memories drift as you slumber.”

 

 Stacey falls to the floor as your magic strikes out, her tall and gangly body hitting the floor with a hard thump.

 

 You rush over to her, checking her for injuries. Her forehead is swelling up. She’s going to have a nasty bruise, but otherwise she isn’t significantly injured.

 

 You quickly push her to the side of the door, at least out of sight unless anyone enters. Satisfied with your impromptu efforts to conceal her, you only hope that the spell has taken hold on her memories.

Actions

<>
 
 
 
 
 <>

passages.name: CheerleaderIntervention2
passages.content:
“Wait!” you cry out. “I’m just checking the Ward! Christina told me to!”

 

 Reagan maintains her look of distrust, but you sense her magical build-up subsiding. “Is that what you were doing? Checking it?”

 

 You nod. Reagan eyes you carefully.

 

 “I don’t like you,” Reagan says. “And I don’t trust you. But Christina seems to think you are worth it. And she’s the head cheerleader. For now.”

 

 You return her stare, but avoid replying.

 

 “Make sure it’s still working,” she says, before turning at leaving. You watch her go, still sensing the raw power within her.

 

 She could have bested me if that got ugly, you think. I’m going to need help.

 “Fall asleep,” you spit out, “and let your memories drift as you slumber.”

 

 Reagan’s eyes glare at you as you throw every piece of magic you can muster at her. You can feel her putting up a fight with her own power, pushing back. She grits her teeth and you feel her draw upon her own affinity to counter you.

 

 Then, with a brilliant flash of yellow energy, Reagan falls back to the floor.

 

 You walk quickly over to her body. She’s out cold. 

 

 Staring at the unconscious Reagan, you take a deep breath. Tiredness washes over you. The magical wellpool inside yourself feels empty. Drained. You think you managed to wipe her memories. Just.

 

 She almost bested me, you think. I’m going to need help.

 

 Not wanting to stay any longer, you leave Reagan to her slumber.

Actions

<>

passages.name: CheerleaderIntervention3
passages.content:
“Fall asleep,” you cry, “and let your memories drift as you slumber."

 

 
 
 You send a tsunami of magic towards Christina, almost knocking her to the ground. She is quick to put up a defence, as you feel a surge of energy rise up from within her. She grits her teeth, and tries to push back against your relentless assault.

 

 Smiling with grim determination, you press harder. Drawing upon the additional training that Gladys gave you, the well of magical affinity inside your body only grows stronger.

 

 “No!” Christina shouts, as she sees what you are doing. She tries to push back, but a moment later you watch as she flies back to the floor.

 

 You cease your assault, taking a deep breath. Slowly, cautiously even, you walk over to your bested opponent, still feeling a flicker of magical aura within her.

 

 Against all odds, she is still fighting against the spell. She looks up at you with sad eyes. “I thought we’d be friends, Em.” 

 

 Her head then tilts on her neck. She finally falls asleep.

 

 You take another deep breath, centering your energy in the calm and quiet. Silence is only fleeting, however. From outside, you hear a quiet set of footsteps. Approaching.

 
 If Christina is surprised by your boldness, she doesn’t show it. As you crash a wave of magic into her form, you feel a surge of energy rise up from her being to counter. She even grins, drawing deep down in her own well.

 

 You yourself grit your teeth, feeling your wave being turned towards you. Inside your body you draw and draw from your own essence, looking for glimmers of energy that have gone previously untapped. 

 

 It’s just not enough. With a smirk, Christina steps up her ferocity and you lose control, hurtling back, landing on your bum in a heap.

 

 With the slow sound of clicking heels, Christina walks towards you. “When you look back on this, Emily, at least you can say you tried. Unfortunately, as much as I’d like to look past this altercation, I’m afraid I’m out of tim

passages.name: CheerleaderIntervention4
passages.content:
“You’ve been very busy,” he says to you. “A pity to waste such a powerful gift on a hopeless endeavour.”

Reaching down into the magical well inside your body, you see if there’s anything left to throw at him. You stop when he chuckles. 

“Your magic can’t affect me, girl.” He then reaches into his waistcoat pocket and pulls out around something that consumes your eyes. An amulet, near-identical to the one you wear. 

“You’ll find I’m quite well protected against your charms. And the rest of Charlton’s male faculty have similar trinkets to protect them.”

Stitch walks further into the room, giving you a wide berth as he doesn’t take his sharp eyes off of you. “I’m curious, Miss. Phillips. Fiddling with pithy curses seems beneath someone of your power. Is it a coincidence, then, that your sudden appearance occurred just as our plans for this year’s Prom are being finalised? Or is disrupting that also part of your goal?"

 He glances at Christina, sleeping on the floor. “She was a powerful tool,” he tells you. “To best her in the magical arts would have taken great skill. But a tool that can be broken is of no use to anyone.”

 

 He eyes you before continuing. “You must understand that you can’t hope to defeat me. I’ve been here longer than you’ve been alive. And contrary to what you might think, you are not the first rebel with such power.”

 

 “Instead, I propose a deal.” He gestures with his hand back to Christina. “Remove her memories. Take away her recollection of ever being a cheerleader. And take her place as the head of the school coven.”

 

 Stitch stares back at you, unblinking. Waiting for an answer.

 

 Actions

 <>

 <>
 
 He eyes you carefully before continuing. “You have a choice now, Miss. Phillips. One more chance. Either get in-line, accept your fate, and knuckle down. Or the only curses you’ll care about will be the names the reprobates call out to the strippers in Dicks.”

 

 He gives you a final stare, then turns to Christina. “Reinstate the

passages.name: CheerleaderIntervention5
passages.content:
“I’ll take her place,” you whisper. 

 

 Stitch nods, still unsmiling. “Then prove your commitment,” he says, gesturing to Christina. “Be merciless. Give her a life that she would detest.”

 

 You take a deep breath. You focus on Christina’s mind, drawing upon a reservation of magic buried deep in your body. You see her thoughts and feelings as colours and swirls, slowly swirling around in her sleeping mind. The yellow and oranges of a bright, popular and confident cheerleader.

 

 You push hard on her memories. They fracture into a thousand pieces. One word comes to mind. Nerd.

 

 Christina’s elements fuse together again, but as they do so you notice what was once yellow and orange is now brown and green. Clumsy, you think. Unkempt. Unpopular. Magic is just in the books that she devours.

 

 The memory package forms in front of you, solidifying in Christina’s brain. Satisfied, you take a deep breath and relax.

 

 “Good,” Stitch comments. “Now. You will attend the Charlton Hotel tomorrow evening and introduce yourself as the new head of the coven. Select individuals from this school meet there to confirm arrangements for our Prom. You have a lot of work ahead of you, Miss. Phillips. Don’t fail me like her. As for your tampering with our instituions protections against the tainted world, I shall see that they are reset. You of course, will be exempt. Now you will choose your own submission willingly.”

 

 You look at Christina, asleep. You can still see traces of what you have done to her memories. “Yes,” you say. “Headmaster.”

 

 “Now,” Stitch says, looking directly at you. “Go home. Leave the school. Pause and reflect. Think about how best you’re going to serve your school.”

 

 His eyes flick towards the door. Shaking with nerves, you gather your things up, and rush out the door.

 

 Actions

 <>
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 “No,” you tell him, finally finding the courage to speak. “I won’t help you.”

 

 Stitch stares at you, emotionless. “A pity. And

passages.name: Day3
passages.content:
You feel yourself stirring from a dreamless sleep. You slowly open your eyes and look around your room. Your new room. Your new life. Which feels very different to what it did even twenty-four hours ago.

Yesterday's events bubble up to the forefront of your mind. Emily's struggles at school. The amulet. The transformation. The lessons. The teachers.

You take a deep breath as you realise you've run out of time to ponder what you will do next. For the time being you've got to keep up appearances and find a way for Emily to get through the school year so she can leave. And so can you.

One thing is clear. Either you or Emily has to go back to Charlton Upper School. If Emily goes back to school, she'll be susceptible to whatever is going on. How long can she hold out before she is irrevocably changed?

Yet, even putting aside how you feel using the amulet again, there are challenges in you taking her place. You need to retain your job to survive, and Mr. Thompson won’t accept many more sick days from a new employee. Then there’s Jennifer. How could you possibly tell her what you’ve uncovered?

Bracing yourself, you swing your hairy legs over the bed and put your feet on your carpet, ready to face this day. Albeit with trepidation. 

You glance over to your bedside cabinet and see the amulet, it's green emerald shining brightly. Even a metre away, its presence warms your heart and lungs. 

You reach out and put it in your pocket. However you are going to deal with today, you're sure the amulet will play a part. 

**Actions**

<>

passages.name: Act2Prologue
passages.content:
You lie down on your bed, letting hours drift by as you run through the encounter with Stitch. Once you feel the amulet warm, you transform back into your self and hang the amulet on your beside table. Lying back on your bed, soon sleep takes hold of you.

**Actions**

<>

passages.name: Act2Sleep
passages.content:
Your dreams are filled with empty corridors at school, as you run from faceless stalkers, hearing your heels clip-clopping as you fruitlessly try to find an exit to the maze of corridors without end. 

Heels?

Then you realise who you were in your dream.

**Actions**

<>

You jolt awake, bolting up in your bed. You hear the quiet cooing of pigeons from outside and the sunshine pouring in from your windows. Then you notice the shadow cast over your bed. 

An ageing woman, skin withered and cracked, is perched on the end of your bed. She has a gleam to her eyes and a smile that hints at secrets.

Perched on the end of it, smiling with hidden secrets, is Gladys.

“I think we better have a little chat, don’t you?”

passages.name: Act2CharacterScreen
DreamLeafAnnie
passages.content:
**Charlton School Gates**

 [image: ./images/School_Gates.png]

 

 You open your eyes to find yourself standing before the iron arch of Charlton Upper School. Pale silhouettes stream through the gates, uniformed figures whose gray blazers and pleated skirts blur together, faces lost in mist.

 

 You glance down at your own body: the familiar cut of a teenagers uniform hugging your slender frame, a single lock of blonde hair slipping from its ribbon to brush across your vision. You realise, you are Annie. Young, defiant, and on the cusp of everything that changed.

 

 A sharp laugh snaps you back. There, just inside the gate, stands Jennifer. Except, a caricature of her. All athletic poise and perfect grin, clad in the school’s cheerleader outfit. 

 

 She steps forward, arms on hips. “Oh hello, Annie!” she beams. “Remember how we never got along? Because you bullied me. This will be interesting, showing you exactly what you did!” 

 

 “Who are you?” you ask.

 

 She laughs, high pitched and youthful. “I’m the collective memories of Jennifer, of course. This is how everyone remembers her. But we both know that’s not the full truth, don’t we? Come on! We have school to attend.”

 

 Her green eyes gleam as she turns and strides through the gates, inviting you to confront the past.

 

 **Actions**

 <>
 
 **Charlton School - Main Corridor**

 [image: ./images/School_Corridor.png]

 

 You find yourself in the long, gray corridor of Charlton Upper School. Faceless students drift past in uniforms, their footsteps echoing on polished floor.

 

 Ahead, Jennifer stands by her locker. The real Jennifer. Dressed in a daggy school uniform. Younger, more innocent. You notice how pale and tense she is. How her books are held close as if they might shield her from the world.

 

 At her right shoulder, a darker echo of herself shimmers. The caricature. She watches her real self, then turns back at you with a dramatic raised eyebrow.

 

 “What are we doing here?” you whis

passages.name: DreamLeafJennifer
passages.content:
**Charlton School Gates**

 [image: ./images/School_Gates.png]

 

 You blink awake beneath the iron arch of Charlton Upper School. Pale silhouettes drift through the gates. Identical gray blazers and pleated skirts, faces blurred by morning mist.

 

 Your gaze drops to your body: the crisp lines of a student uniform fitted to your curves, a single lock of brunette hair slipping free from its ribbon. In this moment, you are Jennifer. Young, uncertain, poised on the edge of everything that would change.

 

 A tinkling giggle cuts through the hush. There, just inside the gate, stands Annie. Only not the Annie you know but a larger-than-life caricature of her. Her school skirt is scandalously short, her white blouse buttoned low, the heels of her black Mary Jane’s impossibly tall. She bounces forward on tiptoe, blue eyes sparkling.

 

 “Oh, hi Jen!” she purrs with saccharine cheer. “Remember how we never got along? You used to pick on me. This’ll be fun! Showing you exactly what you did!”

 

 You swallow and manage, “Who are you?”

 

 She tosses her hair and giggles again, voice high and breathy. “I’m the sum of everyone’s memories of Annie, silly. The way they all remember her. Coy, over-the-top, a flirt. But we both know that’s not the whole story, don’t we? Come on, let’s explore.”

 
 
 With a flourish, she steps through the gates, beckoning you to follow.

 

 **Actions**

 <>
 
 **Charlton School - School Cafeteria**

 [image: ./images/School_Cafeteria.png]

 

 You step into the bustling cafeteria, gray-uniformed bodies drifting past like restless phantoms. The smell of the food is absent, reminding you this is not reality.

 

 Your eyes settle on Annie, seated alone at a corner table, quietly nibbling her sandwich, every gesture measured and small. Her uniform is regulation, her make-up subtle. She looks more timid and humble than you would have ever dreamed her being.

 

 Beside her perches the caricature of Annie - with her too-short skirt and exaggera

 [image: ./images/Fresh_Pot.png]

 

 Light creeps in from between the Venetian blinds covering the nearby windows to the Fresh Pot cafe, casting a shaft of light across Jennifer’s smiling face. The smile, you note, directed at you.

 

 “I can’t believe you like filter coffee,” she says, an undertone of coyly mocking you in her voice. “I figured big city types preferred oat lattes and fancy espressos?”

 

 “Simple man, simple pleasures,” you reply, giving her a grin. “Besides, I’m more interested in the company than the coffee.”

 

 She says nothing, but you see a slight blush in her cheeks. Inwardly, you high-five yourself. Three dates in and you’re starting to get a handle on what Jennifer likes. Compliments and honesty being at the top of the list.

 

 “Do you think you’ll ever go back to the city?” She asks, before taking a sip of her own drink.

 

 You take a moment to consider your response. Truthfully, Charlton has been hard for you. Your parents unexpectedly left soon after moving here, quickly followed by your brother Brock when he left for college. The town is so different to what you’re used to, without even considering the new responsibility of making sure Emily gets through her final year at school.

 

 In a challenging year, meeting Jennifer whilst clothes shopping for your new job has been one of the few high points. You don’t want to mess this up.

 

 “I don’t know if I’ll stay long term,” you say with consideration. “But there’s a few things that might make me stay.”

 

 Jennifer raises an eyebrow, but her smile grows a little behind her coffee cup. Her eyes meet yours and don’t drift away.

 

 That is, until a firm hand clasps on your shoulder, jolting you in your seat.

 

 **Actions**

 <>

 [image: ./images/CharltonTownHall.png]

 

 The Town Hall lobby is a study in old-world authority - polished wooden floors, solemn paintings of forme

passages.name: Dream1
passages.content:
The moment broken, you turn to look up beside the booth you and Jennifer are sitting in and grimace. Dan grins down at the pair of you.

 

 Something feels off about Dan’s presence. You vaguely remember this date, and you’re certain that Dan didn’t make an appearance. But his repugnant cologne is enough to convince you that he’s here, now and present.

 

 “Phillips,” he mutters, before his eyes flicker over Jennifer. “Well, well. Look at you two. Cozy little date, huh?”

 

 You’ve only known Dan for a few days since starting at Thompson’s Repairs, but it’s been enough time to get a handle on him. So you’re not surprised when his slimy smile hangs as he stares at Jennifer.

 

 “Didn’t think you were Jennifer’s type,” Dan says, amusement clear in his voice. “She always struck me as the kind of girl who’d want a real man in a relationship, not one of those… well, you know.” He gestures vaguely. The intent is clear.

 

 Jennifer stiffens. “Not all men have to be alpha males, Dan,” she says, her voice level. “Some of us actually like men who can hold a conversation instead of just talking up their egos.”

 

 “Is that right?” Dan asks Jennifer, his grin unwavering. “Not what you used to say, Jen. Thought you liked men to be protectors. Like Gordon.”

 

 Jennifer’s eyes furrow at him, her smile long gone. She briefly looks to you, a hint of embarrassment on her face. Clearly though, she’s looking to you for a steer.

 

 “What about you, Phillips?” Dan says, his hand still resting on your shoulder. “Do you subscribe to all that modern talk?”

 

 Actions

 <>

 <>

 You swivel your torso enough that Dan loses his grip on your shoulder, almost sending him falling to the floor. You can’t help but grin as he loses balance.

 

 “There’s a time and a place for it,” you tell him. “But men should protect their girl. Sometimes, the old ways are the best.”

 

 You glance at Jennifer to see her reaction. Her eyes are wide and open with shock. There’s a hint of a grin to her

passages.name: Dream2
passages.content:
As you flip through the pages, your confidence wanes. The questions are strangely worded, almost deliberately confusing.

 

 Do you affirm your household’s commitment to the values of Charlton’s Civic and Domestic Order?

 

 That’s not what you expected for a school form.

 

 Which of the following best describes your home: a) Traditional Household, b) Transitional Household, c) Unstructured Environment?

 

 What does that mean?

 

 In the event of educational concerns, which household member is designated to liaise with the town’s guidance committee? a) Father, b) Male Guardian, c) Husband, d) Other (explain).

 

 Why is “Mother” not on this list?

 

 Your head starts to ache.

 

 The receptionist, watching you struggle, offers a thin smile.

 

 “You seem a little lost, dear,” she says, tone perfectly neutral. “Not everyone is suited for this kind of work, but that’s why Charlton has such capable men around to take care of it.”

 

 You swallow hard, as she waits for your reply.

 

 Actions

 <>

 <>

 You grip the pen and force yourself to push through. But the longer you work, the harder it gets.

 

 The words blur together. The questions feel like riddles, twisting in ways that make no sense.

 

 You get through barely half of it before the receptionist casually leans over the counter, glancing at your messy handwriting.

 

 “Oh, sweetheart,” she says, shaking her head. “Some people just aren’t made for forms, are they?”

 

 You let out a nervous little laugh, feeling your face heat up.

 

 “Oh, don’t worry,” she continues, her voice warm, almost maternal. “You don’t have to be good at this. Some people contribute in other ways. A little charm, a bright smile - that can be just as valuable.”

 

 You giggle. A high, breathy sound that isn’t yours.

 

 The moment it escapes, you freeze. *Did you just titter. Like a girl?*

 

 Before you can recover, she plucks the half-finished forms from your hands.

 

 “I’ll see what we can do,” she says, alre

passages.name: Dream3
passages.content:
Brock exhales sharply, taking your attention back. “Look,” he says, tone shifting. “I just mean you need to start thinking long-term. University starts next week. You’re staying here. I’m leaving.”

 

 He glances at Emily and then gestures at her. “She’s not exactly fitting in,” he says. “Charlton expects girls to be girls. Into fashion and makeup. Not reading books all the time. ”

 

 Emily freezes for a second, her hand tightening around the mascara wand. 

 

 “She needs some help.”

 

 You pause, glancing at Emily. She doesn’t say anything, but you can see it - the small, defensive shift in her shoulders, the way she won’t meet your eyes.

 

 Brock claps you on the shoulder, gives you a knowing look.

 

 “Plus, the house is a mess.” His tone is light, but the weight in it is impossible to ignore. “You need to start looking after things around here.”

 

 Then, just before he turns to leave, he tosses out one last line over his shoulder:

 

 “Just try to take some accountability. Clean up or help Emily. I don’t care. But do something.”

 

 And then he’s gone, grabbing his jacket and heading out for one last drinking night before university.

 

 You watch him go. The door shuts with a finality that lingers.

 

 You turn back to the kitchen, to Emily, to the mess. And you realize you’re not seeing Jennifer tonight. The question is, what are you going to do?

 

 Actions

 <>

 <>

 You exhale slowly, looking around the cluttered kitchen.

 

 Emily barely glances at you, still poking at her mascara like it personally offended her.

 

 Letting her attempt her make-up routine, you grab a dish towel and get to work. The first few minutes feel forced, like you’re only doing this because Brock got in your head.

 

 But as you wipe down the counters, as you stack the plates, as you sweep the floor, something shifts. You start noticing little things that need fixing.

 

 A smudge on the fridge handle? You clean it.

 

 A cushion on the couch slightly askew? Y

passages.name: Dream4
passages.content:
You turn your head, blinking in surprise. A senior manager, a man in his mid-40s with a tailored suit and an air of calculated importance, is standing by the glass-walled meeting room.

 

 You hesitate, then push back from your desk and walk over.

 

 “Sir?”

 

 He barely looks at you. “The board’s about to meet, and we need coffee for the clients. Make it quick.”

 

 You stare at him.

 

 “Coffee?”

 

 He finally looks at you - more impatient than annoyed, like you’re a slow student failing a simple test.

 

 “There’s a Priority One issue in the system,” you explain, straightening your back. “I was just about to -”

 

 He waves a hand, dismissing your words like they don’t matter.

 

 “Yes, yes, the system’s down. And you’re part of the team, so you’ll have plenty of time to do your part later.” His expression sharpens. “But clients keep this company running, and right now, their meeting is more important than whatever you're doing at that desk.”

 

 You open your mouth to argue, but he tilts his head, as if reconsidering something.

 

 “Unless, of course, you’d rather take this attitude to HR?”

 

 The implied threat hangs heavy in the air.

 

 You feel it deep in your gut - that quiet certainty that the wrong response here could set the tone for your entire career.

 

 And you are not in a position of power. Not yet.

 
 
 Actions

 <>

 <> 

 Your jaw tightens, but you nod.

 

 “Yes, sir.”

 

 The manager smiles - just a small, satisfied smirk - before turning back to the meeting room.

 

 Your flats - you wore flats today, didn’t you? - barely make a sound against the floor as you hurry to the break room, grabbing the tray and carefully balancing the steaming cups.

 

 By the time you return, the meeting has already begun.

 

 You knock, step inside, and quietly place the drinks in front of the senior executives. None of them acknowledge you. Not a nod, not a thank you. You are invisible.

 

 Leaving the room, you rush to your desk and log i

passages.name: Dream5
passages.content:
Already flushed from alcohol, Jeanette’s dark curls bounce as she drags you toward the cluster of people lounging near the table. She’s wearing a fitted crop top with high-waisted jeans, a beer bottle in one hand, the other clutching you like you might vanish if she lets go.

 

 “Where have you been?” she demands, her smoky eyes narrowing playfully. “Don’t tell me you were, like, actually studying or something.”

 

 “She was probably being responsible,” another voice teases - a girl named Lila, curled up in the corner of the couch, nursing a drink.

 

 Jeanette snorts. “Well, she’s making up for it tonight.”

 

 A red cup appears in your hand, and before you can object, Jeanette nudges it toward your lips.

 

 “Come on, babe. No excuses.”

 

 The others grin, their laughter warm, expectant.

 

 “You don’t want to be the only one sober, right?”

 

 Her voice is sweet, persuasive, and there’s something so easy about giving in. A bottle of something strong is passed around, the rim glistening in the party lights.

 

 Jeanette hooks an arm around your shoulders, her grin mischievous. “I was starting to think you’d gone boring on me.”

 

 The warmth of her presence, the energy of the group, it all pulls you in -

 

 Then a shadow falls over you.

 
 
 Jeanette looks up, her expression shifting, amusement giving way to something else.

 

 You sense the presence behind you before you even turn around.

 
 
 Actions

 <>
 
 A low chuckle, close enough that you feel the heat of someone standing behind you. You glance over your shoulder -

 

 And he’s there. Tall. Confident. The kind of effortless charm that makes a man magnetic without even trying.

 

 A button-down, sleeves rolled up to his elbows, collar open just enough to look relaxed but deliberate. He’s got a beer in one hand, a half-smirk playing at his lips.

 

 And his eyes are on you. “, right?”

 

 His voice is smooth, like he already knows the answer. You nod - you think? - before he continues, hi

passages.name: Dream6
passages.content:
The room erupts into applause as you stand.

 

 The fabric of your graduation gown swishes around your legs, the smooth material brushing against your bare skin as you move.

 

 Your heels click against the polished wood of the stage, the sound steady, confident.

 

 You ascend the steps as your heart beats with exhilaration, the bright stage lights casting a glow over everything.

 

 And then you are there, standing in front of the headmaster.

 

 A warm smile, a firm handshake.

 

 He places the diploma in your hands, his grip steady, his voice quiet but unmistakable.

 

 "Congratulations, Miss Phillips," he murmurs, just beneath the hum of the crowd.

 

 You turn to face the applauding audience. From next to you, you hear the headmaster add quietly. "But remember our little deal. Did you honor our agreement?"

 

 And then --

 

 His hand rests on your bum.

 

 A light touch, deliberate but unseen, out of view from the cameras and the beaming crowd, from your smiling family in the audience.

 

 For a moment, just a flicker of a second, something feels off. Because you remember your graduation.Don’t you?

 

 Yes, your family was there. The applause, the warm light, the sense of accomplishment - that was all real.

 

 But, this? This moment? It happened like this?

 

 Had he done that?

 

 The thought lingers, unformed, unspoken. And your thoughts return to the hand on your arse.

 
 
 Actions

 <>

 <> 

 Your breath steadies as your fingers tighten around your diploma. You earned this achievement, fair and square.

 

 The feeling of a hand on your rear fades. You glance down, noticing the headmaster is standing at least a foot away from you. His hands tucked behind his back. Smiling back at you with genuine pride. From a headmaster to a hard-working student.

 

 The camera flashes, the crowd claps, and when you look back to the audience, your family is still beaming at you.

 

 Pride radiates from their faces, from their belief in you, from the

passages.name: EmilyGradesEvents
passages.content:
"Splendid!” you hear the voice say. “I'll see you at the school shortly. It's crucial we discuss Emily’s future sooner rather than later."

 

 She promptly hangs up. You look at Emily briefly. This must be a good thing, right?

 

 **Actions**

 <>
 

 "Oh, that's unfortunate,” the voice replies. “Emily has so much potential; delays can be detrimental. Please make an appointment soon. Time waits for no one, after all."

 

 You hear her hang up. You look at Emily, briefly. This must be a good thing, right? When you have a free morning, you make a note to swing past Charlton and see this career advisor.

 

 **Actions**

 <>
 

 **Charlton School - Main Corridor**

 [image: ./images/School_Corridor.png]

 

 You make your way through Charlton’s streets, stepping into the corridors of Charlton Upper School. At the reception desk, a bright, bubbly young woman greets you warmly but professionally.

 

 “Good morning!” she says with a friendly smile. “You must be Phillips? Mrs. Carmichael is expecting you. I’ll take you to her office.”

 

 She quickly stands and leads you down the hallway, her heels clicking softly against the polished tile floor. “Right this way.”

 

 She pauses outside a polished oak door and gives it a brief knock before ushering you inside, offering one final reassuring nod before walking back towards reception.

 

 **Actions**

 <>
 

 "Of course," you reply. "Anything to support Emily."

 

 "Oh, wonderful!" The receptionist responds enthusiastically. "I'll let Mr. Stitch know. He'll expect Emily on Tuesday lunchtimes. He's extremely busy, you know, so he only offers these sessions to our most promising students. It's quite an honor."

 

 Her voice softens slightly, adding with gentle insistence, "Please remind Emily that Mr. Stitch's time is very valuable, so she should make every effort to attend."

 

 Before you can respond further, she cheerily thanks you again and hangs up.

passages.name: MagazinePumpingIron
passages.content:
You flick through the magazine, finding the lead article. Curiosity piqued, you begin to read it.

**Pumping Iron**

*Max Caress, the school drop out who made millions lifting weights*

Written by Oliver Oakwood

The article wastes no time in setting the tone. Men today have gone soft, it declares. Too many excuses, too much thinking, not enough doing. That’s why Max Caress made it and why everyone else is still stuck in mediocrity.

Max wasn’t some academic. He didn’t waste his youth buried in books or chasing pointless qualifications. He dropped out of school, picked up a set of weights, and never looked back. While others sat in classrooms debating theories, he was in the gym, pushing himself past his limits, forging himself into something better.

And it paid off. Millions in sponsorships, a personal brand built on sweat and discipline. Thinking never made a man rich - action did. Overanalyzing, hesitating, wondering about the ‘right’ path? That’s weakness. That’s what keeps men from reaching their potential. If you aren’t pushing your body to the limit, if you aren’t proving your strength every single day, then you’re failing.

The message is clear: a real man doesn’t waste time questioning himself. He acts. He builds. He conquers. Anything else is softness, and softness has no place in success.

As you finish the article, you crack your knuckles. Your muscles feel tight, your posture shifts. The words linger, reshaping something inside you. Intelligence? Overrated. What’s the point of all that thinking if it doesn’t get results? No one respects a man who dithers over decisions, who worries about the ‘right’ answer like some nervous schoolgirl. Strength, determination, action - that’s what matters.

And softness? There’s no room for it. No room for self-doubt, no room for weakness, no room for anything that isn’t hard-earned and battle-tested. You don’t just feel different- you are different. Stronger. Better. Any thoughts of feminine urges drift away.

 
 By t

passages.name: MagazineCorporateLadder
passages.content:
You flick through the magazine, finding the lead article. Curiosity piqued, you begin to read it.

**8 ways to climb the corporate ladder**

*Super charge your career in six months by telling her no*

Written by Oliver Oakwood

The article doesn’t waste time with pleasantries. Success isn’t about balance, it’s about priorities. And too many men let themselves get bogged down with things that don’t matter - household chores, date nights, making sure she feels appreciated.

That’s not how winners operate.

A man who wants to climb the corporate ladder needs to focus on one thing - his career. That means cutting out the distractions. Taking out the trash, fixing things around the house, running errands. These aren’t your responsibilities. They’re just obstacles between you and success. The men at the top? They don’t waste their time on things that don’t make them money.

And what about her? If she doesn’t like it? If she starts nagging about quality time or helping out? Tell her no.

Because here’s the secret - the moment you stop catering to her, the moment you put yourself first, that’s when she’ll start chasing you. Women don’t want a man who bends over backward to make them happy. They want a man who’s going places. A man who commands respect, who focuses on his own success.

And if she doesn’t? If she walks? Then she was never worth your time to begin with. A man on his way to the top will always have options. A man, just like you.

The words settle in, clear and undeniable. The idea of putting a relationship before your ambitions seems laughable now. Why waste time proving yourself to one woman when success will bring plenty more? The less you give, the more they want. It’s not about keeping her happy - it’s about making sure you get ahead. And once you do? You’ll have all the choices you could ever want.

 
 By the time you finish the article, you feel the magic from the article seeping through your body. On some level you are aware of the changes it’s caused, b

passages.name: MagazineSuccessWorkplace
passages.content:
You flick through the magazine, finding the lead article. Curiosity piqued, you begin to read it.

**Success in the workplace**

*Tips to keep your mind sharp at work and keep your boss happy*

Written by Sally Hope

You turn the pages carefully, the paper thin and delicate from age. The article wastes no time, launching straight into its thesis: success isn’t just about talent - it’s about perception. A woman must always present herself as poised, agreeable, and - above all - efficient. The text seems to thrum with energy, the words shifting slightly, as if reaching into you.

The more you read, the clearer it becomes. It’s not just about working hard; it’s about ensuring your boss sees you as indispensable. Strategies are outlined in crisp, authoritative language. Be accommodating. Anticipate his needs before he even voices them. Keep your emotions measured, your disagreements subtle.

A strange clarity settles over you. These tactics - manipulative, precise - feel undeniably effective. You feel your mind sharpening, calculations forming before you even realize. Yet, as you absorb the knowledge, a part of you flinches. This isn’t about empowerment - it’s about perfecting submission in the name of professional success.

 
 By the time you finish the article, you feel the magic from the article seeping through your body. On some level you are aware of the changes it’s caused, but no matter how hard you try, you can’t seem to identify what exactly is different within you. Whatever has occurred, it’s part of you now, for good and bad.

 

 Glancing at the other magazines, you are sure of one thing. Whatever magic is present in their pages, it will be more of the same.

 
 By the time you finish the article, you feel the magic from the article seeping through your body. You again can’t identify what exactly is different within you, but you know something has changed.

**Actions**

<>

passages.name: MagazineKeepHim
passages.content:
You flick through the magazine, finding the lead article. Curiosity piqued, you begin to read it.

**Keep him on his toes**

*8 ways to take the lead and keep him keen*

Written by Sally Hope

The article opens with a simple truth: a woman must always remain interesting to keep a man’s attention. A man’s love, it suggests, is not something to be taken for granted - it must be nurtured, maintained, earned.

The strategies it lays out are clear. Never be predictable. Keep him guessing. Show confidence, but not too much - no man wants to feel challenged, only intrigued. Make him feel lucky to have you, but never let him believe he has won you entirely. If he becomes too comfortable, too certain, the article warns, he will stop appreciating you.

There are suggestions - arriving fashionably late, letting his calls sit unanswered just long enough, praising him but ensuring he always seeks more of your approval. It is about balance, about playing the role of the perfect woman - assertive enough to be captivating, but always aware that her happiness comes second to his.

A woman who follows these steps, it insists, will always have a man’s attention. She will be adored, cherished. She will remain the center of his world. Because, in the end, that is what matters most.

You lower the magazine slowly, the words still circling in your mind. The logic of it feels… right. Natural. A part of you resists, questions if this is truly how love works, but the idea slips away before you can hold onto it. Already, you find yourself considering - who do you know that needs to be reminded of your value? Who has become too comfortable? The thought isn’t forced, it’s instinctual, effortless.

And why wouldn’t it be? You know now how to keep a man happy.

*Is that right?*

 
 By the time you finish the article, you feel the magic from the article seeping through your body. On some level you are aware of the changes it’s caused, but no matter how hard you try, you can’t seem to identify what

passages.name: MagazineSuccessCareer
passages.content:
You flick through the magazine, finding the lead article. Curiosity piqued, you begin to read it.

**"I chose my career over my man."**

*A model reflects on breaking it off with her fiancé and focusing on London’s fashion scene*

Written by Sally Hope

It was a choice, the article insists. One path led to Charlton, to marriage, to a quiet life where her worth would be measured in the meals she prepared and the children she raised. The other led to the city, to flashing lights and whispered promises, to a world where beauty was currency and every room held someone more powerful, more important than the last.

At first, it was overwhelming. The men who decided her future didn’t ask her opinions, only if she could pout just right. She learned quickly - words were rarely necessary. It was easier, really. Thinking, questioning, hesitating - those things didn’t get a girl anywhere. Far better to smile, to nod, to let others decide. She was admired, envied, desired. It was enough.

She stopped reading, stopped caring about the things that once mattered. Conversations that weren’t about the latest trends or who was at which party felt tedious. Politics, literature, even the simple pleasure of forming her own opinions faded into irrelevance. What use were thoughts when beauty spoke for itself?

The man she left behind, the life she abandoned - it barely lingers. Just the occasional echo, a name she doesn’t quite remember, a face blurred at the edges. But why dwell? The city demands attention, and thinking too much only leads to wrinkles. 

Men are fleeting, dispensable. At best they come and go, whenever she has time. The lights of the camera are what matters. That’s how she makes her money. That’s how she made her career.

You blink at the page, your thoughts slower than they should be, your mind hazy with the weight of its words. Hadn’t you always valued intelligence? The ability to think, to question? But the idea drifts away before you can grasp it, slipping through you

passages.name: ShadowSelf0
passages.content:
[image: ./images/CityNight.png]

You land softly, as if stepping down from a stair you hadn’t realized was there.

The world around you is mist and shadow, a thick fog curling around your ankles, swirling with each step you take forward. It’s eerily silent. But you recognize this place.

The fog thins, revealing a dimly lit street. Storefronts line the sidewalk, their signs flickering faintly. The streetlights cast long, distorted shadows, stretching impossibly far across the cracked pavement.

Your breath catches. This is your old city. The place you used to call home.

Something about it feels off, like someone took your memories and recreated them from an old, faded photograph. The details are slightly wrong, the colours a little too muted.

But before you can digest specifics, you see - no, you sense - something behind you.

You turn to see a man standing there. Tall, somewhere in his mid-twenties, with short hair and a perpetual layer of stubble. Dark eyes. His very presence seems ethereal, as if made up of gas and vapor. His features and clothes are only just defined enough to recognise him as a person.

As to who he is… you have no idea. Or do you?

“Hello ,” he says coldly. “Or is it now? We have a lot to discuss. About us.”

Your mouth drops as realisation hits you. This person. This man. This is - or was, at least - you.

Actions

<>

passages.name: ShadowSelfDialogue
passages.content:
[image: ./images/CityNight.png]

 

 The shimmering grey facade of what you once were stares back at you coldly. “Ask your questions,” he says. “Then, you have a choice.”

 

 **Actions**

 
 <>

 
 <>

 
 <>

 
 <> 
 
 “What are you?” you ask, staring at the jagged lines of his form.

 

 “I’m you,” he says, slyly. “Or at least, whatever is left. Your little trinket might have overwritten reality with this,” he gestures to your body. “But there’s fragments of other people’s memories and perceptions of you lingering on the edges of their minds. I’m just all of those pieces patched together.”

 

 Struggling to understand, you frown. “So, you’re other people’s perception of me?”

 

 “Of what you were,” he replies. “Not what you’ve become. It’s shocking how quickly you’ve accepted all of this, really. I’d call you weak if it wasn’t just insulting me.”

 

 “I don’t remember being… you. At all.” you tell the spectre. 

 

 “Liar liar,” he says, tauntingly. “There’s bits of me still in you. Buried deep, but it’s there. That voice you keep hearing, questioning your actions. “You don’t really want to be doing this, don’t you? That’s me. Coming up for air.”

 

 “But for the most part, I’m kept under the surface,” he continues. “That’s the power of that amulet. That witch led us to believe it just transformed your body. When actually it distorts reality. If it wasn’t for that vial, I would have been drowned forever. You sure didn’t try to keep me around.”

 

 You detect the menace to the shadows voice. It - he? - sounds like he’s barely holding back rage. At what he’s lost. At what you’ve become.

 

 **Actions**

 <>

 You look the visage up and down, trying to understand what you’re seeing. “What happened to you?” you ask. “To us, I mean.”

 

 “Two different questions. Maybe still the same answer, though. You were me when you started using the amulet. . A mid-twenty something programmer who just wanted to protect his sister, get out of Charlton with his girlfriend and

passages.name: ShadowSelfFem
passages.content:
[image: ./images/SportsBar.png]

 

 The air is thick with the scent of beer, sweat, and fried food, the low hum of conversation punctuated by bursts of laughter from nearby tables. Neon signs flicker lazily on the walls, casting a warm amber glow across the bar.

 

 You blink. Something feels off. You can feel the weight of your shoulders, the broader width of your chest, the familiar but now alien sensation of stubble grazing your jaw as you exhale. You glance in the nearby window and catch your reflection staring back at you. The reflection of .

 

 *"What’s wrong?"* a voice murmurs inside your brain. *"Feeling out of place?"*

 

 You swallow, your throat thicker, voice deeper than you remember. You remember this place. A dive bar, back in the city. That your brother Brock and his friends used to frequent. The ones around this table, right now.

 

 Opposite, your brother leans back in his chair, tipping his beer bottle toward you. “You gonna order, man, or what?”

 

 You look up to see a waitress standing patiently beside the table. A table full of Brock and his friends. And now you. As .

 

 She’s young, pretty in a casual, effortless way, her blonde ponytail swinging as she pulls out her notepad, a pen tucked neatly behind her ear. She’s already smiling, friendly but distant, the kind of practiced politeness that comes from waiting tables in places like this.

 

 “What can I get for you boys?” she asks. Boys. The word slips over you strangely, both familiar and wrong.

 

 “Uh, yeah,” you clear your throat. “Can we grab another round of -”

 

 “She’s asking what *you* want, genius,” Brock cuts in, smirking. “Come on, don’t keep the lady waiting.”

 

 *Careful. Wouldn’t want her thinking you’re soft, now, would you?*

 

 You ignore the voice and smile politely at the waitress. “Same as the rest, thanks.”

 

 She nods. “Coming right up.”

 

 That’s it? Where’s the fun in that?

 

 But the conversation moves on before you can dwell.

 

 **Actions**

passages.name: ShadowSelfOri
passages.content:
[image: ./images/StudentApartmentGathering.png]

 
 
 The room is warm, dimly lit by a mix of cheap fairy lights and the glow of a game paused on the TV in the corner. The air is thick with the scent of cheap beer and microwave pizza, voices overlapping in a haze of conversation and laughter.

 

 You blink. The beer bottle in your hand feels solid, familiar. Your clothes fit differently, the looser, more relaxed weight of a hoodie against your arms.

 

 You’re . And this is a memory. It seeps into your brain. A low-key university gathering, not a raging party - your crowd. A handful of guys from your course, a few friends of friends, and Emma, the girl you’ve had your eye on since the start of the semester.

 

 Feels good, doesn’t it? Being yourself again. No makeup, no skirts, no giggling for attention. Just beer, games, and a pretty girl worth chasing.

 

 Emma is sitting on the couch, curled up with her legs tucked under her, laughing at something one of the other guys just said. She’s cute, in a way that feels attainable. Not the kind of girl who’d be out of reach, but someone you could have a real shot with. Maybe even a real thing with.

 

 You’ve also seen the way she glances at you, the small moments where she lingers in conversation.

 

 You can do this.

 

 Yeah, you can. But you have to prove it. Don’t just sit there and let the night pass you by. Show her you’re interested. Show everyone.

 

 **Actions**

 <>

 You move over to the couch, but just as you approach a voice interrupts from behind it.

 

 “Hey, Emma,” Greg says, leaning nervously over the sofa.

 

 Memories swell up. Greg is one of your friends. A decent guy. Geeky, quiet, not blessed with confidence. 

 

 “Oh. Hey Greg,” Emma responds, politely smiling back to him. 

 

 Watching him for a moment, you grimace. Something about the way he’s leaning forward just a little too much, the nervous way his fingers twitch against his bottle. He’s trying to put himself out there. Just like

passages.name: ShadowSelfInt
passages.content:
[image: ./images/OfficeBreakoutSpace.png]

 
 
 The hum of the office is distant, muffled by the walls of the breakout space, where a small team huddles around a whiteboard covered in diagrams and equations.

 

 The air smells of dry-erase markers and stale coffee, the low buzz of conversation punctuated by the occasional laugh or sigh of frustration.

 

 You’re standing with your team, sleeves rolled up, half-drunk coffee in hand, watching as people throw out solutions for the latest issue in your solution design.

 

 This is familiar. You know this space. You know this team. Your team, back in the city, before you moved to Charlton.

 

 *"Feels good, doesn’t it?"* your Shadow Self murmurs, his voice curling at the edges of your mind. *"Being the smartest guy in the room again. Not the cute little office worker waiting for approval.”*

 

 You shift slightly, the familiar weight of your stance, the comfortable press of your shirt collar, the distinct, once-automatic confidence of being in his element.

 

 The whiteboard is a mess of half-formed architecture, lines and notes attempting to solve a thorny bottleneck issue in the latest system rollout.

 

 Everyone’s talking, but no one has the right solution. Then -

 

 “I have an idea.”

 

 A young woman steps forward. You recognise her. Claire.

 

 **Actions**

 <>
 
 You glance at her as she moves towards the whiteboard. She’s eager, pushing past the subtle groans from a few guys who, despite working in tech in the city, still think she’s out of her depth.

 

 But she doesn’t let it shake her. She grabs the marker and launches into an idea.

 

 It’s… smart. Really smart. A novel approach, one that pushes the limits of what the system can handle.

 

 *She’s good. But she’s also wrong.*

 

 You see the flaw immediately. Her solution is too clever for its own good. It’ll create a severe load on server requests, causing bottlenecks elsewhere.

 

 She doesn’t see it. Neither does anyone else. They’re noddi

passages.name: ShadowSelfAss
passages.content:
[image: ./images/HearthAndHome.png]

 

 You’re sitting at a table in The Hearth & Home - Charlton’s finest dining establishment. Where couples celebrate anniversaries, families mark milestones, and the atmosphere itself hums with quiet tradition.

 

 The restaurant is quiet but elegant, the soft glow of candlelight reflecting off polished silverware. The scent of rich sauces and wine lingers in the air, blending with the muted hum of conversation.

 

 Every table is perfectly arranged, white linens crisp and untouched, couples sitting with straight backs, speaking in hushed, polite tones. The waitresses move gracefully, all smiles and pristine uniforms, their hands folded neatly behind their backs when not in use.

 

 The very air here carries an unspoken expectation. Of manners, poise, and tradition.

 

 And yet, the moment feels stifling. You shift slightly in your chair, the collared shirt fitting snugly against your frame, the weight of your old form grounding you. You’re again. And across from you, Jennifer sits, her arms resting neatly on the table, her blue eyes sharp as she watches you.

 

 You somehow recall this moment. You remember this. This was your first couple’s fight.

 

 *"Funny, isn’t it?"* your Shadow Self murmurs, his voice dripping amusement. *"How quickly things fall into place when you’re in the right skin again."*

 

 The candle flickers between you, and Jennifer exhales, shaking her head.

 

 “You keep acting like this is something I can just accept.”

 

 **Actions**

 <>

 “I’m not asking for the world, ,” she tells you, a restraint notable in her voice. “Just that you consider me. I’ve never been anything other than forthcoming about wanting to move from Charlton.”

 

 You feel yourself looking around. People are watching. Not obviously, not overtly, but in the way Charlton does.

 

 You can feel their eyes flicker over to your table, women whispering behind delicate hands, men occasionally glancing between their own conversati

passages.name: WorkPerformanceEvents
passages.content:
**Thompson's Handyman Repairs - Thompson's Office**

 [image: ./images/Thompsons_Office_Thompson.png]

 
A cluttered and cramped office, lined with filing cabinets. An imposing desk is the centerpiece, covered in files and paperwork.

 

 Inside, Thompson drops into his leather chair with a grunt, the worn material groaning under his weight. You take a seat opposite him, the chair height at the lowest setting. You suspect, on purpose.

 

 "Gotta admit, Phillips," he starts, cracking his knuckles behind his head. "When you first showed up, I wasn’t sure you’d hack it. Thought you’d fold under pressure."

 

 A pause. He smirks. "But, I’m the first to admit when I’m wrong."

 

 You offer him a polite smile. He’s actually the last.

 

 "You’ve put in the work. In Charlton, that matters." His gaze sharpens, locking onto you like a challenge. "Hasn’t been easy, has it? Parents off on business, brother gone to Uni. That’s a lot of responsibility for someone in a new town."

 

 You give a slow nod, unsure where this is going.

 

 Thompson exhales through his nose, pulling open a drawer. "But if you pull your weight around here, Charlton looks after its own. Once you're in, you're in. And I’d say you’ve proven yourself enough that it’s time to get in."

 

 He slides a slip of paper across the desk. The ink is slightly smudged, but the address is clear - a house towards the outskirts of town, on the border between the community and the farming fields that surround it.

 

 "Poker night." He says it like that explains everything. "Happens at the Mayor’s place. One of the teachers - Darcy Harrow - organizes it. Just a gathering. Some of the local men, couple of the older students to show them how to play a few rounds. Maybe some beers, cigars." He leans back, watching your reaction.

 

 
 Thompson tilts his head slightly. "Bring your sister along, too. It’s not exclusive for men. We’re an equal opportunity community. Some of the guys bring their girlfriends and wives to

passages.name: AndreaDialogue
passages.content:
“You seem a little on edge,” you say to her. “Is everything okay?”

 

 Her eyes flick back to the room, then to you. “Lennox is being particularly spiteful today," she confides in you. "He's threatened to fire me three times today."

 

 She glances over her shoulder, then sighs. “We’re so understaffed right now,” she says quietly. “I’m trying my best. Mr. Sermon and Lennox won't budge on their strict hiring policy. They've worked their way through almost every unemployed girl in town."

 

 "What are they looking for?" you ask.

 

 "Young. Pretty. *Compliant*," she replies. "Unless you know of anyone new to town, I don't think I'm getting a break."

 
 
 
 “Planning a trip?” you ask her. She gives you a puzzled look.

 

 “Why would I do that?” Andrea asks you. “Foreign countries are dangerous.”
 
 
 “Planning a trip?” you ask her. You notice a little grin form underneath the nervousness.

 

 “I’d love to,” Andrea tells you. “I know they told at school other countries are dangerous, but I still want to go on a plane. Just once.”

 
 

 You hear a distant clatter from the kitchen, but it’s enough to make her jump. “I’m sorry!” she stammers. “I should get back to it. Unless there's anything else?”
 
 
 “You seem a little on edge,” you say to her. “Is everything okay?”

 

 Her eyes flick back to the room, then to you. “Yeah, everything is fine. Is there anything I can help you with?”

 She again looks around, then sighs. “We’re just a bit understaffed right now,” she says quietly. “I’m trying my best. Please don’t tell Pierre or Lennox. Hopefully they’ll find some more help soon. Maybe then I can take a break.”

 Annie gives you a look. "I think we should have a chat, first of all," she says, the demeanour of a bright and bubbly secretary disappearing. "Come over one morning before work. We can talk."
