passages.name: BritneyDialogue
passages.content:
"I was curious about who had my place on the squad before me," you begin. "Tandi. At least, I think that was her name."

 

 Britney shares a look with Stacey before turning back to you. "Who's been talking about Tandi?"

 

 You shrug. "Just heard the name mentioned a few times."

 

 "Tandi was - is - someone you don't want to get caught up with," Britney tells you, for once losing her sparkle. "She's toxic, Em. Got into all kinds of bad stuff."

 

 "Or bad stuff gets into her," Stacey snipes. "Regularly."

 

 Britney shushes the cheerleader, then smiles at you. "She used to be on the squad. But Em -" she hesitates, then lowers her voice. "She's turned into a total slut!"

 

 Stacey rolls her eyes at Britney's anxiety about talking this way. "Stop it!" Britney says to her friend. "She has! Last I heard, she hangs out with those icky bikers outside of town."

 

 "What happened to her?" you ask.

 

 Again, Britney seems uncomfortable. "Just didn't belong in the cheerleaders. But I'm sure that won't happen to you, Em. You belong with us!"

 

 You notice Stacey giving another dark glance to her friend. It's enough to make Britney fall silent.

 

 Actions

 <> 

 "Curses?" Britney’s eyes light up with excitement, and she leans in closer. "Oh my gosh, they’re, like, so fascinating! Did you know you can use them to make people totally do what you want? Like, Reagan was being super bossy the other day, and I thought, Wouldn’t it be hilarious if she couldn’t stop hiccupping during practice?" She giggles, the thought clearly delighting her.

 

 "Of course, Reagan didn't let that go unanswered," Stacey follows up, causing Britney to pout. After glaring at her friend, she turns back to you.

 

 "They’re kind of like this secret weapon, you know? A way to, like, set things right when people are being... difficult. And they’re fun to experiment with! You wouldn’t believe how many tiny changes can totally mess with someone’s day." She twirls a strand of her hair, her to

passages.name: CarmichaelDialogue
passages.content:
You force a smile, nodding your acceptance. "That sounds like a wonderful opportunity for Emily. I'm sure she'll appreciate the experience."

 

 Lydia’s smile broadens subtly, a gleam of approval flickering in her calculating eyes. "Splendid decision. Thompson’s Repairs will provide her with precisely the experience she needs to excel after school. Mr. Thompson will be pleased."

 

 She scribbles something neatly onto a notepad before placing her pen carefully down. "I'll inform Emily immediately. I'm certain she'll make the most of this opportunity. Have a good day, . Phillips. I’ll make sure Emily is aware she is expected at Thompson’s Repairs each Friday afternoon."

 

 You nod, holding back your feelings, and exit the office. You push back the thoughts that come to mind, of what Emily will experience working as a secretary. Hoping that you’ve made the right decision.

 

 Thoughts turn to Emily and how she will react. You might want to talk to her before she goes to school next and finds out for herself.

 

 **Actions**

 <>

 You clear your throat gently, meeting Lydia's gaze with practiced calm. "I appreciate the offer, but I'd prefer Emily focus on her academic achievements for now."

 

 Lydia’s smile dims noticeably, her eyes narrowing for just a fleeting instant before returning to composed professionalism. "Oh, I see. While academic excellence is admirable, practical experience is also essential for our students here in Charlton. Real-world responsibilities shape character, after all."

 

 “I understand,” you reply. “But I want to give Emily the best opportunity to graduate with grades that reflect her abilities.”

 

 The career advisor gives you a polite smile, but you sense something underneath it.

 

 
 ”I commend you for your commitment to academic resilience,” she says. “In fact, perhaps we can help spur Emily on. Our cheerleaders are granted certain perks for their extra-curricular activities. We can rescind these, however, if we feel the stu

passages.name: CaseyDialogue
passages.content:
“You seem like you’re not from around here,” you tell Casey.

 

 He takes a drag from his rollie and chuckles. “What gave it away?”

 

 You glance at the cigarette, causing him to laugh further. You grimace when the laughter inevitably turns to coughing.

 

 “I was born and bred in Hackney,” Casey tells you. “Back before it all turned into fancy flats and coffee shops. Moved here about ten years ago. Wanted a quiet life. Didn’t know how quiet things could get.”

 

 “Why Charlton?” you ask him. 

 

 “I was a boxer,” he replies after he takes a drag. “Almost went pro, didn’t quite make it. Had a nasty blow in the ring and Doctor said I needed a holiday. Ended up breaking down just down the road and spent the week here getting the motor fixed. Liked the quiet life so much that when I got back to the big smoke, it felt like poison every time I took a breath.”

 

 He puts his roll-up in his mouth and inhales. “Irony and such like,” he mutters as smoke drifts out of his mouth. “I know.”

 

 You nod, watching the roll-up burn. “What do you think of it? The town, I mean.” 

 

 Casey grins wryly. “Well I must not mind it too much if I spend a decade here.” After another drag, he continues. “It’s like going back to when I was younger. Some of that’s good. Some of it’s bad. All things being squared, I think it balances out.“

 

 Actions

 <>

 “I’m interested in signing up for classes,” you tell Casey. “What do you have here?”

 

 “Boxing for the blokes, ballet for the girls,” Casey sighs, blowing out smoke through his nostrils. "Flo tried to start up some yoga, but that business with the karate chops put the skids on it.”

 

 “Karate chopping?” you ask.

 

 He sniggers, and coughs from the action. “Yeah, got an old mate of mine to come up from London. Teach some of the kids some self-defense. Only they got carried away and judo chopped John Kaplar’s fence.” He pauses to take a drag from his roll-up, then continues. “Then that Owen Oakswood made a big song and danc

passages.name: ChristinaDialogue
passages.content:
“I was wondering if you think I’ve done enough?” you ask her. “If I’ve proved I have what it takes.”

 

 
 
 ”You’ve been a busy bee, haven’t you?” Christina says with hushed tones. “I’ve been hearing all around the school of some unfortunate souls finally being put in their place.”

 

 She leans into you, flicking her blonde hair over her shoulder. “Nice work.”

 

 She glances back at the cheerleaders momentarily, before turning her whole body to face you. “I think you’re ready, Emily. Really ready. Tell you what. Come see us at the school gym after school. We’ll have your initiation ready.” 

 

 Her blue eyes sparkle with mischief, but she says nothing more and turns back to the cheerleader practice. 

 

 **Actions**

 <>
 
 
 
 
 
 
 ”Keep at it, Emily,” Christina tells you. “We don’t just let anyone climb the Charlton social ladder, after all.”

 

 She smiles conspiratorially, then turns her attention back to the cheerleaders. It seems you’ll have to do more.

 

 **Actions**

 <>
 

 “Hey Christina," you say. "I need a favour."

 
 
 Christina glances back to the other girls practicing, then focuses on you. "Sure Emily. We've got to look after each other, after all. How can I help?"

 
 
 You take a step closer, lowering your voice. “I need you to cause a distraction after school. Enough to make Prichard leave her classroom.”

 

 Christina grins, raising an eyebrow. "My, you're being inventive already, Emily. Good work."

 

 You feel yourself smiling at the compliment. "Thank you."

 

 "Are you sneaking in after she leaves?" Christina eyes you curiously. "Or are you planning on being held back after school?"

 

 "Detention," you admit to her.

 

 She nods. "Sound plan. Just be careful. Prichard's punishments are very forceful. Plus, you're also a cheerleader now, Emily. You have a reputation to uphold. Detention is not a good look for our team."

 

 "I'll be discreet," you assure her.

 

 "Good." Chrissie appears to mull over something before conti

passages.name: CliveDialogue
passages.content:
“You went to Charlton Upper School, didn’t you?” you ask him. “What did you think of it?”

 

 “It was pretty swell, as school goes,” he says. “Used to play a bit of rugby. Still can throw a ball with the best of them.” He flashes you a smile.

 

 “Did you ever notice anything strange going on?” you try, pressing him.

 

 “No more than anywhere else. Bit of bullying, bit of favouritism. Some social darwinism, if you get my meaning. I bet you would have ranked top of the food chain when you were at school, that right darlin'?"

 

 His disarming smile diminishes your eye rolling. "Did you notice anything odd?" you press on. "About the lessons?”

 

 “Yeah. Lots of equations. Couldn’t understand any of them. Never used one since. I'm more of a do'er and a thinker than a fancy academic. Algebra never impressed a pretty lady.” His eyes sparkle as he looks at you.
 
 

 Clive scoops up some of the tools and puts them back in the kit. “As much as I like spending time talking to beautiful girls, I got a job to get to. Tell you what, though. Come by McTafey’s in the evening and I'll buy you a drink. Then I'll have all the time in the world for you."

 

 Clive gives you yet another smile and returns to sorting through his equipment, leaving you watching him.
 
 
 “You went to Charlton Upper School, didn’t you?” you ask him. “What did you think of it?”

 

 “It was pretty swell, as school goes,” he says. “Used to play a bit of rugby. Still can throw a ball with the best of them.” He flashes you a smile.

 

 “Did you ever notice anything strange going on?” you try, pressing him.

 

 “No more than anywhere else. Bit of bullying, bit of favouritism. Some social darwinism, if you get my meaning. I bet you would have ranked top of the food chain when you were at school, that right darlin'?"

 

 His disarming smile diminishes your eye rolling. "Why’s that?" he continues, moving on. "Your sister having a hard time there?”

 

 You nod, just a little. “A little,” you tell him.

passages.name: DanDialogue
passages.content:
"Oh, I always enjoy serving you, Dan," you tell him, leaning over slightly and smiling. 

 

 He grins and puts down a wad of notes on the payment platter. You lean over slowly and pick them up, allowing his eyes to gaze on your breasts.

 

 “Keep the change, hun,” he tells you. “And if you ever feel like serving me something more spicy, you should come see me one night in McTafeys. I’d love to get a drink with you.”

 

 You smile coyly at him and walk off, swaying your hips as you clip-clop back around the counter. He leaves shortly afterwards, flashing you a grin.

 

 When he leaves, you count the money and are shocked at the size of his tip. You consider why you encouraged him. It was just for the tip, right?

 

 Though your mind returns periodically to the encounter, and you can’t help but smile when you recall how he looked at you.

 

 Actions

 <>

 <>

 You lean forward, trying to channel every ounce of charm you think Florence would have. "Oh, Dan," you say, grinning too widely, "I always enjoy... um, serving you. You’re... really good at... ordering coffee."

 

 Dan stops mid-count, his fingers hovering over the wad of notes as he stares at you. His brow furrows slightly, his grin faltering. "Uh... thanks, I guess?" he says, clearly confused.

 

 You panic and lean in closer, lowering your voice in what you think is a seductive tone but comes out more like a weird growl. "Maybe one day... I could... pour you an extra hot coffee. Just for you."

 

 Dan blinks, his mouth slightly open. The silence stretches unbearably as a nearby patron coughs awkwardly. Then, Dan lets out a laugh so loud and abrupt it startles the table behind him. "Wow, Florence, you’ve got... something, alright!" he says, grinning but clearly not sure if you’re joking or serious.

 

 To your horror, he tosses a single coin onto the payment platter with a loud clink. "There’s a tip for making me laugh, sweetheart," he says, shaking his head in disbelief. "And hey, if you ever figur

passages.name: DeBussyDialogue
passages.content:
“Mrs. DeBussy,” you begin, measured and respectful. “I need access to Terrence Handle’s locker.”

 

 She turns to you, eyes blinking quickly in confusion and then sharpening with interest. “Emily, darling. That’s a rather... unusual request, don’t you think?”

 

 You keep your expression level. “I have reason to believe he may be in possession of something inappropriate. Something that contradicts the standards we hold here at Charlton.”

 

 Her smile tightens into that familiar, thin line. “You’re suggesting… contraband?”

 

 You nod. “Yes. Something in his biology notebook. I think it could be worth investigating. I thought it best to come to you before it became more… public.”

 

 DeBussy tuts softly, as if the very notion has already inconvenienced her. “Normally, I’d have to speak to Headmaster Stitch. Locker searches are not typically within my remit.” Her voice rises slightly, just enough for the rest of the club to overhear.

 

 You lean in, tone hushed. “This is sensitive. And time-sensitive. If there’s something in that book, I need to see it before he hides it or destroys it. I wouldn’t ask if I didn’t think it was important.”

 

 She considers you for a long moment, then sighs in that dramatic, theatrical way she reserves for self-congratulation. “Very well. You’ve been a tremendous help to the club. And I suppose, as President, a certain... discretion must be afforded.”

 

 From beneath her desk drawer, she retrieves a small brass key, dangling it in front of you for a beat too long.

 

 “Straight there and back. And I expect a full report on what you find.” Her smile returns to its usual sugary position, still soured at the edges. “It’s such a delight when our brightest girls take initiative.”

 

 You take the key, pocketing it with a nod. “You’ll have your report.”

 

 “See that I do, dear,” she says, already scribbling in her notebook again.

 

 Actions

 <>

 
 “Can you tell me a little bit about the lessons you teach here?” you ask DeBu

passages.name: DennisDialogue
passages.content:
“Dennis,” you begin. “I want to apologise. I haven’t been nice to you.”

 

 “What do you want, Emily?” he says, not looking up at you.

 

 “I just wanted to say that I think I was mean to you because I actually like you,” you continue. “I think I got carried away.”

 

 He looks up at you, then looks back to what he’s doing. “You are making fun of me.”

 

 You realise you’re going to have to do more than speak to him. You step towards him, cautiously stroking his arm. “No, I mean it. I like intelligent guys.”

 

 His eyes flick between your face and your hand on his arm. You catch him even looking at your breasts. He gulps. “I don’t believe you.”

 

 You look at him, feeling his conflict. You know you’re going to have to give a final push to get him over the line. 

 

 Grasping his hand gently, you move it around your waist and against your skirt. “Would I do this if I wasn’t serious?” 

 

 You jump a little as you feel his hands clasp your bum, giving it a gentle pinch. His hand darts back and he refuses to meet your eyes with his, but you see a little smile on his face as he returns to what he was doing.

 

 The faint sting of his pinch lingers below your skirt. You know you have won him back over, but more pressing is that the sensation of that sting has brought a smile to your own face.

 

 **Actions**

 <>
 
 You clear your throat, glancing nervously at Dennis. "Uh, hey, Dennis," you start, your voice a little too high-pitched. "You’re, um... good with all this AV stuff. It’s... impressive."

 

 Dennis doesn’t look up, his attention fixed on the camera. "Thanks," he says flatly. "What do you need? Is something broken?"

 

 "No, no, nothing’s broken," you say quickly, shifting awkwardly from foot to foot. "I just thought, you know... maybe we could, uh... talk? Or something."

 

 Dennis glances at you briefly, frowning. "Talk? About what? You need help setting up a projector or something?"

 

 You force a laugh, your cheeks burning. "No, I mean... I

passages.name: ElizabethDialogue
passages.content:
“Hey Liz, I need a favour,” you tell her.

 

 She looks at you from over the rim of her glasses. “Sure,” she says after a moment. “What’s up?”

 

 You take a step closer, lowering your voice. “I need you to cause a distraction after school. Enough to make Prichard leave her classroom.”

 

 A half-smile rises up on Liz’s face. “Do I want to know why?”

 

 You match her expression. “Let’s just say there’s something that needs investigating in there. Something very cursed.”

 
 
 
 Her half-smile twitches with excitement. “A curse? Is that what’s causing it?” You nod and her eyes gleam.

 

 “And you can remove it?” 

 

 You nod again. “So you'll help?"

 

 She holds your stare for a moment, her smile thinning. “Say I do. Can you teach me how to lift the curses too?”

 

 “It’s more that I know how to change them,” you try to explain, but this only encourages her. 

 

 “Even better! Think what we can do if we both know how to do that! Come on, *Emily*. Share the knowledge!” 

 

 You stare at her, almost blinded by beaming enthusiasm. You could really do with Liz’ help, but do you think she should know more magic?

 

 
 
 **Actions**

 <>

 <> 
 
 But regardless of whether you would teach her, you can't. You tell her honestly that you don't know how to perform the curse. 

 

 "Well, you best get learning it then!" she replies, smiling. "I'm sure our mutual friend at the junk shop might be able to help. You should go see her. We're not exactly on great terms - she thinks I don't take these things seriously enough. But I'm sure she'll teach you!"
 
 

 **Actions**

 <>
 
 
 A slight frown appears on Liz's face. "A curse? What do you mean?"

 

 The young woman stares back at you, blankly. She has no idea what a curse is, as she has no idea about magic.

 

 "It's not important," you deflect. "I just need to get into the classroom. If I were to find myself in detention, do you think you could distract Prichard for a while?"

 

 "You? In detention?" Elizabeth loo

passages.name: EmilyDialogue
passages.content:
“Can you help me do my make-up?” you ask her. 

 

 Emily raises an eyebrow. "This is a role reversal. Until recently I was asking you."

 

 "You know your face better," you reply.

 

 She shrugs. "Okay. Let's go to the bathroom. This better be worth it."

 
 “Can you help me do my make-up?” you ask her. 

 

 For a moment she just stares at you. “Fine. Let’s go to the bathroom. This had better be worth it.”

 
 
 
 You follow her as she leads you to the bathroom. She instructs you to sit on the bath rim and she takes out a number of products from the cabinet.

 

 For the next fifteen minutes, Emily applies a variety of products to your face. She works quickly and silently, having done this time and time before. You sit passively as creams are worked across your face, powder is peppered over your skin and gloss is pressed against your lips.

 

 “Hold still,” she says when you squirm. “We’re almost done.”

 

 Finally, Emily steps back and looks at you. “There. Take a look.” She nods towards the bathroom mirror.

 

 You stand up and glance at your reflection. The face of Emily stares back at you, but it’s not the image you’ve become accustomed to. Her eyes look large and radiant. Her lips are glossy and pronounced. Her skin is smooth and clear of the blemishes you’ve noticed as you’ve inhabited her body.

 

 You look cute.

 

 *Or do you mean sexy?*

 

 **Actions**

 <>

 
 “Do you think you could help me pick out some clothes?” you say to her quietly.

 

 She smirks a little. "You might recognise a few things. I haven't got a lot that you didn't hand down to me."

 

 "At least I'll like them", you reply.

 

 Emily shrugs. "Let's go have a look in wardrobe, then."

 
 “Do you think you could help me pick out some clothes?” you say to her quietly.

 

 She rolls her eyes. “You’re really going all in on this aren’t you? Fine. Let’s go to my room.”

 
 

 You follow Emily into her bedroom and sit down on her bed as she looks through her wardrobe. “If it were

passages.name: FlorenceDialogue
passages.content:
“What’s that you’re reading?” you ask Florence.

 

 “Oh this?” Florence says, gesturing to the paperback on the counter. “It’s nothing. Something silly that I’ve been trying to read for the last year.”

 

 You glance down at the cover, noting the title. *“Executive Success - throw punches like a girl in the boardroom.”*

 

 “It's not about travel or other countries," she replies. "So I don't know if it's your thing. But I can't give up being interested in businesses. Even small shops like this one. I know it's stupid. Men make better leaders? Numbers and books are beyond us.”

 

 You follow her plucked eyebrows as they glance at the book. “I keep trying to understand it though. One day, maybe something will stick.”

 

 Looking at the book with interest, it seems personal to her. You could probably use it to power the amulet, if you could get your hands on it.

 You glance down at the cover, noting the title. *“Executive Success - throw punches like a girl in the boardroom.”*

passages.name: GladysDialogue
passages.content:
She chuckles. “I am Gladys. Just an owner of a junk shop. Nothing more than that, I’m afraid.”

 

 
 “You’re lying,” you reply. “I’ve never seen this place before and my friend lives opposite it.”
 
 
 “You’re lying,” you reply. “I’ve never seen this place before and my girlfriend lives opposite it.”

 
 

 “Maybe you weren’t looking for it,” she tells you. “It has a funny habit of only attracting those who already know about it. I should really do some marketing, but you can’t teach an old dog new tricks. At least, not whilst they're old.”

 

 
 
 “I did some research,” you try. “And this shop came up. It said you might be able to help me.”

 
 “I was told about you,” you try. “And this shop. They said you might be able to help me.”

 
 

 Gladys grins. “That I can. But you can help an old lady in asking the right questions. Who I am is incidental. What I can tell you is another matter.”

 

 Actions

 <>

 “So. Just to be clear,” you tell Gladys. “Magic is real.”

 

 She laughs. “Yes, dear. By the fact you are here, you must have experienced enough to know that. But that is not your real question, is it?”

 

 You give her a look. “How does it work?”

 

 Gladys smiles. “I haven’t the foggiest. It is something deep-rooted in certain people, attached to their very souls. I have a bit of it attached to me, in spite of what face I am wearing. A condition that I’m sure you can sympathise with, can you not?”

 

 “It does have its limitations,” she continues. “It can be found in anyone, but only a woman can use it. Men are susceptible to it, can be blessed or cursed by it, can use its charmed trinkets. But only a woman can create. Or a soul in a woman’s body, to be specific. A hunch says that may be important to you.”

 

 She looks you up and down. “I can teach you, if you like. If that’s why you’re here.”

 

 Actions

 <>

 “There’s something strange happening at Charlton School,” you tell her. “I was told you could tell me about it.”

passages.name: GordonDialogue
passages.content:
“I was thinking about joining a gym,” you tell him. “You’re a member of one, aren’t you?”

 

 Gordon nods, grabbing a box beside his vehicle. “Casey’s. Off Orchid Road. You should come. Never too late to get in shape.”

 

 As if demonstrating his own level of fitness, he throws the bulky box into the van. You know you wouldn't be able to lift it.

 

 
 “Look pretty flexible already. So we can start you with some weights. Then cardio. Soon you’ll be long-distance running. Plenty of clubs run through the fields outside of town. Can do personal sessions, if you like. Like I do for your friend, Jennifer.”

 

 "You teach Jennifer privately?" you ask.
 
 
 “Look pretty flexible already. So we can start you with some weights. Then cardio. Soon you’ll be long-distance running. Plenty of clubs run through the fields outside of town. Can do personal sessions, if you like. Like I do for Chambers. You know her?”

 

 "You teach Jennifer Chambers privately?" you ask.

 
 

 He nods. “She stops by the gym sometimes,” he replies. “Works next door at the department store.”
 
 

 Gordon continues to load his van. You eye him carefully, noticing how he appraises your body without hiding it.

 

 “Stop by in the evening,” Gordon tells you. “We’ll get you fixed up.”

 "How do you know Jennifer?" you ask, caught off-guard by Gordon mentioning her.

passages.name: HarrowDialogue
passages.content:
You raise your hand quickly before the lesson begins. Harrow catches sight of you immediately, flashing a gleaming smile. “Yes, Miss Phillips?” he says, voice warm and theatrical. “What could possibly be more compelling than the marvels of natural biology?”

 

 You hesitate under his gaze. “I’d like to be excused from the lesson.”

 

 He tilts his head slightly. “Excused?” he repeats, the smile never faltering. “Miss Phillips, surely you’re not suggesting you’d rather skip today’s fascinating dive into the instincts of the natural world? The primal elegance of procreation, the biological imperatives of the female form.” He spreads his arms wide, like a magician revealing a final trick. “There’s so much to admire.”

 

 You shift in your seat. His words are friendly enough, but something in his tone makes it hard to push back. His charm is thick. Heavy. And somehow oppressive.

 

 Still, you take a breath. You need to see the boy’s version of the segregated lessons.

 

 **Actions**

 
 <>

 <>

 <>

 You reach into your bag and carefully withdraw the hall pass. Harrow’s smile freezes for a split second. His eyes narrow as he takes in the slip, then flick up to meet yours.

 

 “Well,” he says, the warmth in his voice not quite masking the suspicion beneath. “It appears you’ve been given permission for other priorities.”

 

 He steps aside slightly, folding his hands behind his back. “Though I must ask. Are you quite sure, Miss Phillips? Today’s lesson is especially illuminating.”

 

 You nod, even as something in the pit of your stomach makes you hesitate. A quiet itch to stay and watch.

 

 Harrow’s smile returns. “Very well. Hurry along then. We wouldn’t want to deprive your other commitments of your… presence.” He gestures toward the door with a graceful wave. “I’ll see you next time.”

 

 **Actions**

 <>
 
 
 
 

 You shift slightly in your seat, fidgeting. “I’m sorry, sir,” you say, keeping your voice low. “I think I might need to be excused. It’s… wome

passages.name: HeatherDialogue
passages.content:
“What are you doing waiting here, Heather?” you ask her. 

 

 She looks off to the side, reflecting, before turning back to you and whispering softly. “Coach Beach said that I might as well clean every evening after classes. I always come last in PE, anyway. This way, I’ll get extra credit for cleaning. I’ll at least pass.”

 

 “Where’s Coach Beach now?” you ask her.

 

 She shrugs. “Sometimes he comes,” she says quietly. “Sometimes he doesn’t. He gave me a key to the gym. He lets me come and go as I please. I even clean in the mornings too.”

 

 “You sound like you enjoy it,” you tell her.

 

 She looks up at you with her large eyes, a smile appearing in spite of clear reluctance. “You get used to it. The smell. The scents. The boys.”

 

 You let one of your eyebrows rise up. “The boys?”

 

 Heather nods, replying in a hushed, if a little resigned, tone. “Beach lets his football players watch sometimes. Comment. Not that I blame them. After all, girls are made for boys.”

passages.name: JackieDialogue
passages.content:
“Can I have something else?” you ask. You notice a few students behind you roll their eyes.

 

 “Nurse’s orders, Phillips,” Jackie replies sternly. “You get what you got.”

 

 You look down at the bland salad. Your stomach rumbles, demanding food. 

 
 “Can I have something else?” you ask. You notice a few students behind you roll their eyes.

 

 “Nurse’s orders, Mannet,” Jackie replies sternly. “You get what you got.”

 

 You look down at the steak, dripping with fat. Your stomach rumbles, even in spite of how tough the meat looks.
 
 
 “Can I have something else?” you ask. You notice a few students behind you look at you in confusion.

 

 “Nurse’s orders, Duncan,” Jackie replies sternly. “You get what you got. Most kids would kill for it, too.”

 

 You look down at the burger, dripping with fat. Your stomach rumbles, even in spite of how it looks.

 <> 

 “Who makes the lunch?” you ask Jackie. 

 

 She sighs with irritation. “Lunch is prepared in the morning on-site, made specifically as per Nurse Rancy’s direction. If you don’t like it, take it up with her. Now, anything else you want to nit-pick?”

 

 Jackie looks at you, impatiently. Other students mutter behind you in the queue.

 <>

 "Hey Jackie," you begin. "I was wondering if I could borrow the key to the kitchen -"

 

 "Let me stop you right there," she says, her voice clipped and short. "That key? It’s staying right here with me." She gestures sharply at her pocket, her fingers curling protectively.

 

 "Do you have any idea what would happen if I handed it out? Chaos. Kids messing with the ovens, adding hot sauce to the soup. I’ve seen it all before.

 

 "Nurse's orders. Food is as per her instruction. No exceptions. If you're looking to stage some kind of prank, find another way. I’m not playing."

 

 Actions
 
 <>

 "I really need to get into the kitchen," you try. "It's important. I promise I'm not up to mischief."

passages.name: JakeDialogue
passages.content:
“So, you’ve been here a few years," you say, only partially being successful in trying to sound casual. “What do you think about the school?”

 

 “What do you want me to say?” he replies, dismissively. "A school's a school."

 

 You nod and choose your next words carefully. “It just seems very old fashioned.”

 

 “Good,” he snorts. “The more of that, the better. Dad always says that this town is the last haven from hippies and progressives that haven’t ruined this country. I’m glad that someone realises that things are how they are because that’s just the way things are natural.”

 

 “Have you noticed anything strange going on?” you ask, trying to change tactic.

 

 “Only that nerd Dennis taking photos of girls,” Jake says. “I’ve even seen him go down to the basement and hang out with that janitor. Probably jacking each other off over his pictures.”

 

 **Actions**

 <>

 “I was wondering if you could help me with something,” you say to him. “Why there are some lessons that are only for girls.”

 

 He snorts. “There’s things that we need to be taught about how to be a man,” he says. “You wouldn’t understand, sweet cheeks.”

 

 You let the comment slide. *If only he knew.* “I’m serious - what happens in your single sex lessons?”

 

 “So am I,” he replies. “Men don’t need to know about trivial things like I’m sure they teach you about. You wouldn’t want to be in those lessons. They get rowdy.” He smirks, taking a step further towards you. “Or maybe that’s just the type of thing that turns you on.”

 

 You feel his panting from his recent workout washing against your neck. You look up at him, feeling small as he leans over. You look at the worn grass, avoiding his eyes.

 

 He laughs. “You’re such a nerd, Emily. But if you really want to scratch that itch, stop by. You’ll need a hall pass from one of the teachers. Surprised you haven’t got one, already. Thought you only got to the top of the class by having lessons with Stitch. And he gives them out to his f

passages.name: JenniferDialogue
passages.content:
“Hey! Can you stop cooking for a second?” you ask. “I've got something on my mind.”

 

 She turns to look at you, then seeing your serious face, puts the knife down. “Okay,” she says. “That sounds ominous.” 

 

 You look at your friend, still smiling as she waits for you to speak. You wonder whether talking to her about any of what’s happening to you is a good idea. You have no idea how she will take it, if she’ll believe you.
 
 
 “Jennifer, can you stop cooking for a second?” you ask. “We need to talk.”

 

 She turns to look at you, then seeing your serious face, puts the knife down. “Okay,” she says. “That sounds ominous.” 

 

 You look at your girlfriend, still smiling as she waits for you to speak. You wonder whether talking to her about any of what’s happening to you is a good idea. You have no idea how she will take it, if she’ll believe you.

 <>

 
 <>

 
 <>

 <>
 

 
 
 Jennifer stares back at you, waiting for you to speak.
 
 
 “Hey,” you say calmly. “I think we need to talk.”

 

 Slowly, Jennifer looks back at you. “What do you mean?”

 

 You sigh. “There's some stuff going on with Emily at school. And I think you know more than you're letting on. About what went on when you were at school.”

 

 She sighs, putting a knife down on the kitchen worktop. “Are you accusing me of lying, ?”

 

 “I thought we were friends, Jen,” you tell her. “That school is strange. Emily’s having a hard time there. And I think you know more than you’re letting on.”

 

 “Fine,” she says, quietly. “What do you want to know?”
 
 
 “Jennifer,” you say calmly. “I thought we trusted each other.”

 

 Slowly, your girlfriend looks back at you. “What do you mean?”

 

 “I mean, you’re not telling me the full truth about what you were like at school.”

 

 She sighs, putting a knife down on the kitchen worktop. “What does it matter, ? Why do you care what I was like back then?”

 

 “Because of Emily,” you tell her. “That school is strange, Jen. Emily’s h

passages.name: LennoxDialogue
passages.content:
"Andrea told me that you're looking for work?" you tell him. "I was wondering if you had more details."

 

 Lennox frowns. "Andrea? Oh, Miss. Cinders. Do you mean to tell me she's been fraternising with guests?"

 

 You shake your head. "I just asked her a question about it."

 

 Lennox tuts, but returns his stare to you. His eyes drift over your body. A thin smile passes across his lips. "Well. Regardless of how you discovered it, we do have a vacancy. Are you over eighteen?"

 

 You nod. "Just finishing my final year at Charlton."

 

 A thin smile passes across his lips. "I'm sure you are an exemplary student."

 

 "We need a cleaning assistant to help Miss. Cinders. Lord knows she needs the help. We wouldn't want to jeopardise your studies, but if you're willing to work weekends and late nights, there may be a role for you here."

 

 You nod. "How do I apply?"

 

 He looks at you, his eyes cautiously wandering over your face. "No time like the present. Do you have some time free now? I think we should have a little chat. To assess your suitability for the role."

 

 The question is, do you really need to do this? Is access to the hotel really worth that much to you? Or are you that strapped for cash?

 

 *Because you wouldn't have any other reasons for applying for a maid job, would you?*

passages.name: LogainDialogue
passages.content:
“You go to Charlton, right?” you ask him.

 

 “As little as I can,” he replies. “But there’s not much else to do in our sleepy little town, so sometimes I grace them with my presence.”

 

 “How do you find it?”

 

 “Stimulating. Invigorating. The centre of my world.” 

 

 He looks at you, waiting for you to respond. “You’re Phillips’ older sister, right? She’s in some of my classes. She's fun.”

 

 You give him a look. "Easy, no offense meant. She's just cute, is all."

 

 You look at him, feeling odd to be discussing your sister in this way. *Especially as recently your sister has been you.* 

 

 “Now, if you don’t mind,” Logain says, gesturing away. “I have things to do.”

 
 “You go to Charlton, right?” you ask him.

 

 “As little as I can,” he replies. “But there’s not much else to do in our sleepy little town, so sometimes I grace them with my presence.”

 “Stimulating. Invigorating. The centre of my world.” 

 

 He looks at you, waiting for you to respond. “You’re Phillips’ older brother, right? She’s in some of my classes. Cute, in an earnest sort of way.”

 

 You look at him, feeling odd to be discussing your sister in this way. *Especially as recently your sister has been you.* 

 

 “Now, if you don’t mind,” Logain says, gesturing away. “I have things to do.”

 
 

 Actions

 <>

 “So,” you begin. “Why are you out of school?”

 

 Logain grins. “Our illustrious leader Mr. Stitch gave me permission to leave school early. Special privileges, like all the top performers. Cheerleaders, geeks, jocks. He seems to think it's a reward for keeping in line.” Logain snorts. “Letting us wander this dead town aimlessly. Such a generous guy.”

 

 You tilt your head a little. “And which group do you figure you fall into?”

 

 Logain’s grin widens. “Family connections,” he replies, voice laced with sarcasm. “My old man would rather I bunk off with his blessing than end up in detention.”
 
 <>

passages.name: MacDialogue
passages.content:
"I'm Emily," you say, trying to keep your voice high and perky.

 

 "Still Mac," he replies without looking up.

 

 You take a deep breath, trying again. “So you're a talent scout?”
 
 

 “Yup,” he says, finally looking up with a sigh. “Used to be a football player myself. Now stuck coming to small towns like this.”

 

 You give him a smile, trying to warm him to you. "So you're looking for new talent?"

 

 He nods. "Now I spend my afternoons looking at skinny kids trying to impress the camera. And my evenings in the hotel looking at the photos of them."

 
 "I'm Dennis," you say, trying to keep your voice from shaking.

 

 "Still Mac," he replies without looking up.

 

 You take a deep breath, trying again. “So you're a talent scout?”
 
 

 “Yup,” he says, finally looking up with a sigh. “Used to be a football player myself. Now stuck coming to small towns like this.”

 

 You give him a smile, trying to keep the nerves at bay. "So you're looking for new talent?"

 

 He nods. "Now I spend my afternoons looking at skinny kids trying to impress the camera. And my evenings in the hotel looking at the photos of them."
 
 
 "I'm Jake," you say, hearing your voice low and gruff.

 

 "I know who you are," he replies without looking up.

 

 You take a deep breath, trying again. “So you're a talent scout?”
 
 

 “Yup,” he says, finally looking up with a sigh. “Used to be a football player myself. Now stuck coming to small towns like this.”

 

 You give him a grin, trying to warm him to you. "So you're looking for new talent?"

 

 He nods. "Now I spend my afternoons looking at skinny kids trying to impress the camera. And my evenings in the hotel looking at the photos of them."

 
 

 **Actions**

 <>

 You glance around him, taking in the photos spread across the bleachers. "Are these photos of the school football team?” you ask.

 

 “Yup,” he replies, barely looking up. “I’m in town to see if there’s any diamonds in the rough.”

 

 "Anyone standing out?"

passages.name: MartinDialogue
passages.content:
Whether it’s due to Martin’s imposing physicality or his domineering personality, something inside you prevents you from standing up to him. You instead freeze, as the strong brute pulls you against his body.

 

 “Looks like you do,” Martin taunts. “What do you say, darl? My van is parked out on the street. Want to see what a real man looks like?”

 

 Images flash up in your mind. Martin’s van parked in a dark alley. The imposing drunk brute towering over you. The sort of things that you can only imagine he’s insinuating in his offer for fun.

 

 “Think you should leave the girl alone,” you hear a voice say from behind you. 

 

 A hand reaches in and twists Martin’s arm, instantly forcing him to let go of you. You look up to see Clive, holding Martin back with one arm.

 

 “What’s the matter, Burtle?” Martin spits at him. “Got a soft spot for her?”

 

 Clive stares back at Martin, not flinching. “Go. Sit.”

 

 "It's okay," you interject. "I don't mind."

 

 Noise in the bar has fallen away to silence. You take a step back, free from Martin's grasp, looking straight back at him. He grins.

 

 Out of the corner of your eye, you see Clive bristle. "Well don't treat ladies like that in here," he says after a moment. "Maybe try downstairs, Martin. Either way, take your business elsewhere. I think I speak for everyone when we say we don't want to see you back here for a while."

 

 After a tense moment, Martin snorts. “I’ll see you later,” he says to you, winking. He then walks out.

 

 The tense atmosphere diffused, you hear conversation start up again and soon the bar returns to normal. Clive stands by you, only turning his eyes to you once Martin has left.

 

 “You okay?” he asks you. 

 

 You nod. “Yes.”

 

 He studies you for a moment, before grimacing. “You do you, darlin'. But my advice - avoid that one. The drink doesn’t mix well with him.”

 

 Clive smiles with concern, then returns to his own seat, leaving you alone once more.

 
 Whether it’s due

passages.name: MaryDialogue
passages.content:
You clear your throat and offer a tentative smile. “I imagine Sunday mornings must be busy for you now, with Terrence’s having to get ready.”

 

 Mary’s brow creases in a gentle frown. “Oh on the contrary, you’ve taken a weight off my weekend,” she says. “With Terrence in the library with you until lunchtime, it gives me a chance to clean his room. Teenage boy’s bedrooms can always do with a clean.”

 

 
 
 *Success! Jennifer seems to be tutoring Terrence for his biology classes on Sunday mornings in the library. You should go to the library at this time to find out what exactly she’s up to.*

 

 **Actions**
 
 <>
 
 *Jennifer seems to be spending Sunday mornings with Terrence in the library. You just need to find out why.*

 

 **Actions**

 <> 
 

 You clear your throat and ask too bluntly, “So, Mary. What do you do on Sunday mornings?”

 

 Mary frowns. "Mainly I catch up on cleaning and housework. Why?"

 

 A flicker of suspicion passes through her eyes. You haven’t learned anything of value and she’s growing wary of your questions.

 

 **Actions**
 
 <> 

 You offer Mary a gentle smile. “How’s Terrence doing these days?”

 

 Her expression softens. “He’s… improved, actually. Calmer in the mornings, less on edge. Your tutoring has made all the difference. He used to wake up worried about his biology lessons, pacing the hallway before breakfast. Now he doesn’t. Or at least, not so much.”

 

 
 
 *Success! Jennifer seems to be tutoring Terrence for his biology classes on Sunday mornings in the library. You should go to the library at this time to find out what exactly she’s up to.*

passages.name: MichelleDialogue
passages.content:
“What are you looking at?” you ask her. 

 

 She smiles widely and her face lights up at the chance to talk about what’s on her mind. “Just a little window shopping! The Groomed Gal is now offering spa treatments. I can't believe we've got this in Charlton!” 

 

 She turns to the window and points to a handwritten poster taped to the window. “Look. Manicures. Pedicures. Facials. The full treatment!” Even without a make-over, Michelle is already glowing. “I wish I could afford it. Only yesterday I found a pimple underneath my nose. It almost ruined my morning.”

 

 “Looking to treat yourself?” you ask her, nodding to the advert.

 

 She winces. “Yeah. But it’s so expensive. I wish I could afford it. Rhiannon always likes a gossip. People tell hairdressers everything.” 

 

 You look back into the salon, with newfound interest. Michelle makes a point. You might learn a thing or two from spending some time at the salon.

 

 *Because that’s the only reason you’d want a spa treatment, isn’t it?*
 
 
 “What are you looking at?” you ask her. 

 

 She blushes a little, her demeanor a marked change from the lively burst of energy that’s apparent when it’s Emily speaking to her. “Oh. Just window shopping. It's silly, I know."

 

 You glance at the Salon, noticing a handwritten poster pasted in the window. It advertises a new service, full Spa Treatment. Manicures. Pedicures. Facials. And more!

 

 “Looking to treat yourself?” you ask Michelle, nodding to the advert.

 

 She smiles at you, embarrassed. “Yeah. But it’s so expensive. I wish I could afford it. Rhiannon’s always nice to talk with.” 

 

 "Full of gossip too," she adds, before seeing the look on your face. “Not that that interests me, of course.”

 

 You look back into the salon, with newfound interest. Michelle makes a point. You might learn a thing or two from spending some time at the salon.

 

 *Because that’s the only reason you’d want a spa treatment, isn’t it?*

passages.name: OlafDialogue
passages.content:
“Where did you get those photos?” you ask him directly. 

 

 Olaf’s eyes flick for a moment from you to the photos. When his eyes return, he smiles sardonically. “Some I take. Some Dennis takes. Lots of sluts at this school. Lots that will pose for photos.”

 

 His eyes wander over your body, but his expression is cold and hard.

 
 You clear your throat, trying to sound casual. “What about photos?”

 

 Olaf’s grin widens, yellow teeth catching in the low light. He leans forward, spitting on the ground between his boots.

 

 “Our deal,” he rasps. “You give me snaps. I give you keys. Access to places you shouldn’t be going. Then you tuck your camera into dark corners. See what no one else sees.”

 

 His eyes glimmer with a knowing leer. “And I get my souvenirs. For ma' keys. So, you got something for me?”

 

 For a moment, your mind flicks to the photograph of Emily tucked away in your bag. But you couldn't trade Olaf for it. You won't be able to transform into Dennis.

 

 You shake your head. “I don’t have any more.”

 

 Olaf snorts, disappointed, and leans back against the wall. “Come back when you’ve got swapsies,” he sneers. His stained fingernail scrapes idly at the boiler pipe, as though he’s already lost interest.

 
 “You take all these?” you ask, gesturing to the wall of photos.

 

 Olaf’s jaw tightens. His eyes flick from you to the pictures, then back again. “Some,” he mutters. “None of your business."

 

 "What is this - a private collection?" you ask, a hint of hostility creeping into your voice.

 

 "Don’t pretend you don’t enjoy the view,” Olaf spits back. “Quarterbacks get their kicks. I just get what they see.”

 

 "I bet the teachers wouldn't like it, if they found out," you reply, smirking at him.

 

 But instead of growing wary at your subtle threat, he sniggers. "Teachers don't care what I do here. Helps them, even. Identifying the sluts of the year."

 

 Olaf glances back at the photos and you see you've temporarily lost him to day

passages.name: PrichardDialogue
passages.content:
You raise your hand before the lesson begins. Prichard hasn’t even opened her register yet, but her eyes snap to you like a hawk spotting movement.

 

 “Yes?” she says crisply, voice sharp. “Something to share before you begin?”

 

 You straighten slightly. “I’d like to be excused from the lesson.”

 

 Her expression doesn't shift. “Excused?” she repeats, as if testing the word on her tongue. “This is an important subject for ladies. The dangers of the world will prey on those ignorant to them. Do you consider that knowing this matter is optional to your safety?”

 

 You falter under her stare. There’s no warmth. Only the cold certainty of someone who’s never once allowed nonsense in her classroom.

 

 She continues, voice unchanging. “There is a syllabus, little Miss. There is structure. You will find that women who ignore both often end up lost. I presume you have a reason?”

 

 **Actions**

 
 <>

 <>

 <>

 You reach into your satchel and draw out the hall pass. Prichard’s eyes flick to it, narrowing almost imperceptibly. “Well,” she says, her tone dry. “It seems someone believes you have more important business than learning how to survive the world’s dangers.”

 

 She studies you with the faintest curl of disapproval. “Are you quite certain this is the best use of your time, Miss Phillips? There is much to learn today. Things that might save your life one day.”

 

 You nod, though the certainty doesn’t quite reach your chest. A small part of you itches to stay and follow the rules. But you hold her gaze.

 

 Prichard inhales through her nose and gestures briskly toward the door. “Then go. And do try not to speak to strangers on the way. I’ll see you next lesson.”

 

 **Actions**

 <>
 
 
 
 
 
 

 You lean forward slightly in your chair. “Please, Miss Prichard,” you say quietly. “I think I may need to be excused. It’s… women’s matters.”

 

 Her eyes sharpen. “Speak up, girl.”

 

 You hesitate, then murmur again, even lower, “It’s… cramps. I didn’t

passages.name: RancyDialogue
passages.content:
Nurse Rancy folds her hands behind her back as you shift awkwardly on to the edge of the gurney. She studies you for a moment. “So,” she says calmly. “Why don’t you tell me what seems to be the matter?”

 

 You offer a vague shrug. “It’s probably nothing. Just, you know. Cramps. Or something like that.”

 

 “Mmm.” She doesn’t write anything down. Doesn’t even move.

 

 “I didn’t want to make a fuss,” you add quickly. “I just needed a moment to sit down somewhere. That’s all.”

 

 She tilts her head slightly. “So, no sharp pain? No nausea? No bleeding?”

 

 You blink. “No.”

 

 “Then you came here on your own?”

 

 “Well, no. I mean, sort of. I was let out of class, and then -”

 

 “And then you wandered the halls,” she cuts you off. “Rather than coming here. Until you were escorted by a hall monitor.”

 

 Her voice is calm. Measured. But behind her eyes is something harder.

 

 “Something must be wrong, Emily,” she says. “The body is smarter than the mind. We just have to listen to it. And right now, your body is saying something’s off. That means a physical inspection is required.”

 

 She gestures toward you with a subtle flick of her fingers. “All right, missy,” she says, her voice suddenly firmer, “Let’s get those clothes off so we can see what’s what.”

 
 Nurse Rancy closes her folder without looking up. “Back again,” she says, almost pleasantly. “Still something not quite right, is there?”

 

 You try to reply, but she raises a hand gently. “No need. I’ve seen enough to know when a girl’s body is out of step with her mind. And when she’s telling the truth.”

 

 She steps forward with that same crisp professionalism. “Another physical inspection is required.”

 

 She gestures calmly toward your clothes. “Spit spot. Get those clothes off so we can see what’s what.”

 
 

 Actions

 <>

 <>

 You stiffen on the edge of the gurney. “I’d really rather not,” you say, trying to keep your voice even. “I don’t think that’s necessary.”

 

 Nurse Rancy ti

passages.name: ReaganDialogue
passages.content:
Reagan’s fingers tighten on the edges of the book, her knuckles paling as her grip tenses. “It’s nothing. Just some light reading,” she says, her tone clipped and dismissive. “Not that it’s any of your business.”

 

 Her gaze sharpens, daring you to press further. “Though I suppose someone like you would be curious,” she adds with a mocking smile. “Always poking around in things you don’t understand. But let me save you the effort. It’s not for you. So why don’t you go find something more... suitable for your skill level? Maybe a picture book.”

 

 She flips a page with exaggerated precision, her attention ostensibly back on the text but her body language remaining rigid. “Oh, and before you ask. No, I’m not going to let you borrow it, take a look, or even ask me another question about it. I have work to do, and you’ve already wasted more of my time than you deserve.”

 

 Reagan leans back slightly, her eyes narrowing as she watches your reaction. “Is there anything else, or are we done here? I’m sure you have something marginally more productive to do.”

 
 Reagan’s fingers tighten on the edges of the book, her knuckles paling as her grip tenses. “It’s nothing. Just some light reading,” she says, her tone clipped. But cautious. As if she's scared.

 

 She leans back slightly, her eyes trying to gauge your reaction. But she says nothing more.

 She leans back slightly, her eyes narrowing as she watches your reaction. “Is there anything else, or are we done here? I’m sure you have something marginally more productive to do.”

 You glance at the book in Reagan’s hands. "That looks fascinating," you say. "Mind if I take a look? Maybe even borrow it?"

passages.name: SiobhanDialogue
passages.content:
You narrow your eyes in the dim halo of the lamp. “Who are you?”

 

 For a long moment, she says nothing. Then she leans forward, voice low and teasing. “I’m the shadow at the edge of your questions. The whisper you almost catch but never quite hear.” Her smile gleams in the street light. "Or just a mouth that never quits. What’s in a name, anyway?”

 

 When you press, your voice is firmer. “Tell me.”

 

 She sighs. “You can call me Siobhan. By trade, I work at the carnival. Joined them a few years back when they toured up north. But to you, that doesn’t matter. I’m help, if you want it.”

 

 You lift an eyebrow. “And why are you helping me?”

 

 Her smile widens, unsettling and knowing. She folds her arms beneath the tattered shawl at her shoulders. "Magic isn’t meant to be kept chained up for them toffs and well-connected lot. There’s a whole world out there that can help folk like you. I’m here to free it. If you’ll let me.”

 

 **Actions**

 <>

 Siobhan’s grin quirks sharper when you ask, “What do you mean by help?”

 

 She lets the silence press in, then leans close, voice low. “Help’s a wee window into what they’ll nae speak of. Visions you can’t get by pryin’ at their tongues. I’ve got trinkets that’ll pry open the nights they’d rather forget and show you what lingered afterwards.”

 

 
 “Talked to both of ’em, have ya? Neither gave you what you came for. Ye want to know what happened? Understand Jennifer? Or help Annie remember? Annie can’t recall, and Jennifer won’t confess. But ye needn’t beg ’em. Ye can see it yerself… and set their memories free. Choose: the careful one or the broken one.”

 

 With a graceful flick, she produces a small tin from her clothes and snaps it open. Inside lie two delicate leaf cuttings, each pulsing with a faint, otherworldly light. One is brittle and torn, its edges jagged as though fractured; the other is a perfect maple leaf, its veins glowing like living runes.

 

 “Only one,” she whispers. “Ye have to decide.”

passages.name: StitchDialogue
passages.content:
You compose yourself carefully, meeting Stitch's calculating gaze with practiced confidence. Leaning forward subtly, you soften your voice and offer him a gentle, coaxing smile.

 

 “Mr. Stitch, surely there's some flexibility here? Someone of your experience and authority must understand that Emily just needs a bit more... encouragement to truly excel.” You smile warmly, attempting to project quiet confidence. “Perhaps with your guidance, she can turn things around?”

 

 Stitch’s expression darkens, a flash of genuine irritation flickering across his sharp features. He sighs deeply, clearly unimpressed.

 

 “I expected better from you, Miss Phillips," he says coldly, straightening in his seat. “Such tactics may work with other men in Charlton, but I see right through them. This school demands dignity and respect - traits you evidently struggle with.”

 

 He leans back stiffly, cracking his fingers. “That said, I'll give Emily one final chance. But let me be clear: if this is the example you set, perhaps her academic failures aren't surprising. So consider carefully the consequences of your own actions from this point forward. Charlton does not tolerate such unbecoming behavior.”

 

 You nod stiffly, embarrassment burning your cheeks, as you rise and exit his office, feeling the weight of his judgment - and the knowledge that you've narrowly avoided disaster. Stitch’s final warning echoes ominously: Emily’s future now rests precariously on the edge.

 

 **Actions**

 <>

 You try to gather your confidence, attempting a smile that comes out more awkward than charming. Clearing your throat, your words falter slightly as you fumble for the right approach. “Mr. Stitch...maybe if...I mean, perhaps Emily might do better if you...uh, if she had a second chance? There must be, uh, something I could do to convince you.”

At the mention of the word, Terrence's alert eyes bounce from side to side, looking up and down the street. “Nervous? No reason. Why?”

 

 “You seem a little on edge,” you try. “Is everything alright?”

 

 Terrence gives you a look, indecision clear in his expression. Just as he looks set to speak, a pigeon coos, making him jump.

 

 “It’s nothing,” he replies quickly. “I’m fine.”

 You frown. “I didn't know you liked Chantelle?”

Mountains and rivers aren't as interesting as girls. passages.name: TerryDialogue
passages.content:
You take a steadying breath. "What did you need me for, sir?"

 

 His silver eyebrows lift as he smooths a hand over his unbuttoned jacket. He stands, slowly walking around the desk. Lingering dangerously close to you. “Jenna,” he whispers. “Your performance today is…integral. In fact, your future here may well depend on it.” 

 

 He leans closer, his eyes looking deeply into yours. You push back into the leather chair, trapped between its back and Terry, towering over you. “No need to be fearful,” he says, voice dropping to a conspiratorial whisper. “When we’re done. I’m about to make you a star.”

 

 With a flourish, he spins and whisks off a velvet cloth from a nearby easel… displaying a poster.

 

 [image: ./images/receipts_from_the_heart.png]
 
 
 
 A boyish grin spreads across Terry’s face. “If Selfridges can have a brand voice, why can’t we?” he enthuses. “And… why can’t it be a soprano?”

 

 You stare between Terry and the poster, your brain trying to register what you’re seeing. The stylized soprano clutching a perfume atomizer, the older gentleman gesturing behind her. Did Terry model these on Jennifer and him?

 

 Terry draws you from the image with his enthusiastic gestures, grinning with glee. “Think of it, Jenna! I’ve paid a fortune in focus groups and research. They all say the same things. Get better stock control. Sort out the tills. Mundane. Dull. Boring. I told them to go to hell.”

 

 You watch him pace the room. Almost performing a pitch. Grinning manically. “I’ve been in this business since my father set up this store,” he continues. “I can tell you what Evans' Department Store really needs isn’t better signage or modern payment systems, but…” he pauses briefly, stepping near you again. “… a retail opera experience.” 

 

 And ends his pitch with jazz hands.

passages.name: CurrentStateEnd
passages.content:
Thank you for playing! This has been a big undertaking, expanding out the initial two-day jam into what I envisioned for The Secrets at Charlton School.

Charlton is, and will be for the foreseeable future, a passion project. When I can devote time to it, I will do. There are two more acts planned.

If you like what’s here, please let me know on tfgamessite. I also encourage bugs, typos and needed clarifications to be reported. As the game has blossomed / ballooned into its current form, QA will always be a struggle.

Once again, I hope you enjoyed your time in Charlton, and can't wait to share with you the secrets that are still to be revealed. In the interim, here's a sneak peek at the next act:

passages.name: GameOverEvents
passages.content:
**Your home**

 [image: ./images/Your_Home.png]

 
Your family home has an open-plan kitchen within the living room. A large table separates the kitchen space from the lounge area, which boasts a big sofa and a giant TV at the other end.

 

 
 You rush down the stairs to find Emily standing in the hallway, tears streaming down her face. She shakily holds out a letter to you - the Charlton Upper School crest clear and ominous at the top.

 

 "They've expelled me," she chokes out, her voice trembling with despair.

 

 
 Without hesitation, you step forward and wrap her gently in your arms. Emily collapses into your embrace, sobbing against your shoulder. Her voice cracks as she whispers desperately, "Is there anything we can do? Anything at all?"

 

 You gently stroke her hair, knowing how hard you've both tried. Your heart sinks; you remember Stitch’s warnings all too clearly.

 

 "We'll figure something out," you whisper reassuringly, even as dread settles into your bones.

 
 You reach out instinctively to comfort Emily, but she recoils sharply from your touch.

 

 "Don’t," she hisses bitterly, eyes narrowed with hurt and anger. "This is your fault! You didn't help! You didn't do anything!"

 

 "Emily, that's not true -" you begin, but she's already turning away.

 

 "Don't bother," she snaps. With tears still wet on her cheeks, she grabs her jacket and storms out the front door, slamming it hard enough to make the windows rattle.

 

 You're left standing alone in the hall, the weight of your failures pressing down on your shoulders.

 
 
 
 Groggily hearing downstairs, you hear the catchy, unapologetic refrain ringing clearly: "Can't tell me nothing, gonna live my own way..."

 

 You find Emily in the living room, energetically dancing, completely oblivious to the world around her as she belts out the lyrics, her voice ringing boldly through the house. She notices you entering, flashing a carefree grin and holding up a piece of paper.

 

 "It's finally

You look around the apartment for signs of anything personal. Everything looks stylised and unique to Annie, from the 50s furnishings to the pastel coloured flowers. Nothing, however, seems treasured. 

Until your eyes fall on a photo frame on the shelves, tucked behind a plant. You move over to it, brushing aside the leaves to see more.

The photo is of two girls sitting outside on a beautiful cloudless day, smiling brightly at the camera. One of them you instantly recognise as Annie, although she looks a year or two younger. The other you’ve never seen before. Her dark hair contrasts with her pale skin, her eyes are dark to the point where you can barely separate her pupils from their iris. 

The two of them sit on a grass verge, legs sprawled across each other, their temples leaning against each other. Both look carefree. Happy. Innocent.
 

 “Friend of yours?” you ask Annie, as she continues to mix you both some drinks.

 

 She looks over at you and her eyes linger on the frame for a moment. Then she turns back to cutting up a lime for the gin. “Penny,” she says, her back turned to you. “We went to Charlton together. We were... friends.”

 

 “Just friends?” you ask.

 

 
 Annie stops cutting and turns to face you. Her smile gone, but not replaced with anger or pain. To the best of your reading, she just seems confused. You catch sight of her blushing slightly.

 

 She glances at the frame again, then back to you. “Girls are made for boys, .” she says without feeling.

 

 She looks at you with confused eyes, almost faltering. “Of course,” she says quietly. “That’s what they taught us at school.”

Logain flashes you a cocky smile as you approach him. “Hey squirt,” he says. “Didn’t expect to see you outside on a Saturday night.”

 
 
 Logain snorts as you approach him. “Just what my night needed. An unpleasant surprise.”

 

 He eyes you darkly, as if challenging you to speak. Taking the hint, you leave him alone.
 
 
 
 “Hey doll,” Logain greets you casually. “How’s your night going?”

 
 “Hey chief,” Logain greets you casually. “How’s your night going?”

 
 
 Upon seeing you, Logain's expression hardens. An eyebrow rises, daring you to engage with him. He’s not interested in talking with you. Not wishing to get into a conflict, you back away. 
 
 
 Logain smirks as you approach. "Ah, dad's favourite CCO. How's the night treating you?"

 

 "CCO?" you ask, frowning.

 

 "Chief Coffee Orderer," he replies, grinning. "The old man actually smiles when he talks about you bringing him an espresso in the morning."

 

 "How nice," you say, curtly.

 
 
 
A thought appears to cross his face. "Speaking of," he says. "How about you bring me something? I can make it worth your while."

 
 
 Logain looks at you as you approach. "You need something?" he asks, aggressively.

 

 The lack of his trademark grin tells you that he's not interested in talking to you.
 
 
 The teenager’s blue eyes lock with yours. His eyes narrow slightly.
 
 

 “Evening,” he says to you, guarded. He offers nothing more to build upon. You smile politely at him and step away. Whatever Logain is up to, he clearly doesn’t trust you enough to tell you about it.

passages.content:
**Avian Lane**

[image: ./images/Avian_Lane.png]

In most towns Avian Lane would be no more than an alleyway. The backs of buildings on the main street run down one side. On the other, rundown apartments are housed above a line of shops that are more closed than open.

You step out into the morning light of Avian Lane, quiet and peaceful. Jennifer’s work clothes feel more present than before, uniform hugging your curves, wind breezing against your legs.

You look back up to her living room window. She - you - stands there, smiling. Waves to you.

What have you agreed to?

Your hand goes to the amulet. Empty. Jennifer has drained it. You couldn’t transform if you tried. You could go back in, but even now, you can sense Jennifer’s power emanating from inside her apartment. You don’t know what she’s done since you saw her last, but you’ve never felt so powerless.

Listening to your heels clipping on the pavement, you walk to Jennifer’s work. Your work. For the day ahead working at the Evans' Department Store.

<>

**Avian Lane**

 [image: ./images/Avian_Lane.png]

 
 
 You step out into the morning light of Avian Lane, quiet and peaceful. You feel the wind brush against your exposed thighs, Annie's miniskirt not offering much protection against the elements.

 

 What have you agreed to?

 

 Your hand goes to the amulet. Empty. You couldn’t transform if you tried. And even when it recharges, you'll have to complete the work day as Annie. That's what you promised, after all.

 

 Listening to your high heels clipping on the pavement, you walk towards Thompson's Repair shop.

Emily’s room is decorated with pictures of friends you don't recognise, styled in pinks and plush cushions. A large wardrobe is built into the wall, complete with a giant vanity table that is covered in cosmetics.
 

 [image: ./images/Emilys_Room.png]

 
Emily’s room is decorated with art prints and books in earthy pastel colours. A large wardrobe is built into the wall and a small vanity table rests in a corner, with a modest range of cosmetics.

passages.name: EmilysRoomVanityTable
passages.content:
You step toward Emily’s vanity table, the smooth surface cluttered with cosmetic palettes, brushes, and an assortment of tubes and compacts. A large mirror is propped against the wall, reflecting the soft lighting of the room, making the space feel warmer, more intimate.
 

 You step toward Emily’s vanity table, the smooth surface cluttered with cosmetic palettes, brushes, and an assortment of tubes and compacts. A large mirror is propped against the wall, reflecting the soft lighting of the room, making the space feel warmer, more intimate.

 

 For a moment, you just stare. Make-up was always something Emily had, but you never really paid attention to it before. Now, standing here, there’s so much more than you have seen before.

 

 Your fingers hover over the collection of lipsticks, powders, and mascara, the tools of an everyday ritual you’ve never performed.

 

 You could try it. It would be useful to blend in. To aid your investigations.

 

 And that’s why you are here, right?

**Actions**

<>

<>

passages.name: EmilysRoomMakeup
passages.content:
You pick up a brush, hesitating before dipping it into a powder compact. It looks easy enough, but the moment you try to apply it, the coverage is uneven, the color slightly off. The eyeliner drags, the mascara clumps.

 

 By the time you finish, you look in the mirror and grimace. It’s not great. But it’s a start.

 

 You sigh, reaching for a tissue. Maybe tomorrow, you’ll do better.

 

 Tomorrow?

 

 <> 
 

 **Actions**

 <>
 
 
 
 
 

 This time at the vanity, things go smoother.

 

 You remember how the foundation should be blended, how the eyeliner needs a steady hand. The mascara still smudges a little, but overall, it’s an improvement.

 

 When you glance in the mirror, there’s a difference. You look like yourself, but refined, polished. More put-together.

 

 It feels... nice. Not necessary. But nice.

 

 You smile, watching your reflection look stunning as she does that in the mirror. One more attempt, you think, and you'll be just as good as a professional.

 

 Is that what you want, though?
 
 

 <> 
 

 **Actions**

 <>
 
 
 
 
 
 

 By now, it’s almost automatic.

 

 Your hands move without thinking, each stroke of foundation, blush, and liner feeling as natural as brushing your teeth. You barely have to focus - you just do it.

 

 When you meet your reflection in the mirror, there’s no hesitation. This is just part of your routine now.

 

 Just another day.

 

 Another day of you wearing makeup.

 

 <>

passages.name: EmilysRoomWardrobe
passages.content:
You pull open the wardrobe doors, the faint scent of perfume and fabric softener drifting out as your fingers brush against the hanging clothes inside. The space is tidy, organized, but what catches you off guard is the sheer variety. Far more than you ever expected.

 

 Emily was always practical, a little reserved, but as you scan the collection, you realise something's changed.

 

 The soft flow of dresses, the tailored skirts, the occasional flash of lace or bold colours. It’s more than just everyday wear. Some of these outfits are stylish, even daring, and for the first time, you wonder if Emily was changing before all of this started.

 

 Or if Charlton has just been working hard on her, too.

 
 
 As you open the wardrobe, you immediately notice it. The outfit you wore before is back in its place, neatly folded or hung as if it had never been touched. You hesitate, fingers brushing over the fabric. You didn’t put it back. You were sure of that. But the evidence is right in front of you.

 

 Sighing, you don't understand why you are so surprised. Magic, it seems, continues to have its way with you.
 
 
 You pull open the wardrobe doors, the faint scent of perfume and fabric softener drifting out as your fingers brush against the hanging clothes inside.

 

Actions

 Wear the dress - *You should stay in Emily's uniform during the school day*

 Wear the skirt - *You should stay in Emily's uniform during the school day*

passages.name: EmilysRoomDress
passages.content:
You sift through the wardrobe, fingers brushing against soft, flowing fabric before pulling out a dress. It’s light, simple, the kind of thing Emily would wear on a casual summer day. There’s something about it that feels effortless yet put together.

 

 You hold it up against yourself, tilting your head in consideration. It’s a safe choice. Not too much, not too little. Pretty, but practical.

 

 Setting it aside, you reach down for a pair of flats, the natural companion to the outfit. With everything laid out neatly on the bed, you inhale deeply before starting to change.

 

 As the fabric settles against your skin, the outfit feels… comfortable. Like you belong in it.

 

 And that thought lingers longer than it should.

 
 
 <> 
 
 Slipping into the dress, you barely give it a second thought. The fabric rests comfortably against your skin, the fit familiar, effortless. It’s just another outfit now, something you pull on without thinking, because why wouldn’t you? It’s easy, stylish, and it suits you. 

 
 
 <> 
 
 

 Actions

 <> 

 
 
 Your fingers trail over a few casual tops before stopping on something different. A skirt, fitted but not too tight, stylish without being overly bold. It does look quite short, though.

 

 You pull it from the hanger, holding it up in front of you. It’s not the kind of thing Emily wore all the time, but you'd seen her in it a few times. When she wanted to stand out a little.

 

 You find a flattering top to match, something that fits just right, and lay them both on the bed. The low-cut neckline gives you pause for thought. You know how much will be on display by wearing it. But something draws you to it, all the same.

 

 Reaching down, you pick out a pair of ankle boots with a high heel, completing the look.

 

 As you start to change, the outfit comes together effortlessly. When you finally look in the mirror, there’s something about it that feels… natural.

 

 Like this isn’t a disguise at all.

 

 Like maybe, just

passages.name: EvansDepartmentStoreJenniferDialogue0
passages.content:
Jennifer smiles at you when she sees you. “Hey!” she says, as she continues working. “I didn't expect for you to come visit!”

 

 “Just passing by,” you tell her.

 

 She smiles pleasantly, but her attention is drawn away by a customer coughing politely nearby. “Sorry ma’am. One second,” she tells them, before turning back to you.

 

 “I’m sorry. Busy day. Do you want to come around tonight, though?” she asks. “I'll cook something nice. We can catch-up!”

 

 You smile as Jennifer walks off to deal with the customer. 

 Jennifer smiles at you when she sees you. “Hey!” she says, as she continues working. “This is a nice surprise.”

 

 “Just checking in,” you tell her.

 

 She grins, but her attention is drawn away by a customer coughing politely nearby. “Sorry ma’am. One second,” she tells them, before turning back to you.

 

 “I’m sorry. Busy day. But come around tonight?” she asks. “I’ll make it worth your while. I'll cook something nice.”

 

 You smile as Jennifer walks off to deal with the customer. 

 “Oh hi Em,” Jennifer whispers as you approach. “What brings you to the store today?”

 

 “Just browsing,” you say, a little hesitant to say much in your current form. 

 

 “You should take a look at the clothing department,” Jennifer replies. “There’s some new stock that just arrived.”

 

 You nod, looking over at the racks and mannequins on the other side of the store.

 

 “I’m really busy today, but I’ll see you soon, okay? And tell your sister to come round - I'd love to catch-up!"
 
 “I’m really busy today, but I’ll see you soon, okay? And tell your brother I'm expecting him tonight."

 

 “Okay,” you mumble, not for the first time trying to make sense of the messy situation in your brain. You decide to leave Jennifer alone.

 “Hey Andrea,” Jennifer says as you approach. She looks you up and down, then leans in to whisper. "Are you sure you should be walking around dressed like that? Doesn't Sermon mandate you don't wear your uniform outside?"
